title: What is a design pattern?
intro: A design pattern is a reusable solution to a common software design problem. It is not a library, framework, or copy-paste code — it is a proven way to organise and design software.

sections.heading: Plain definition
sections.body: A design pattern is a named, idiomatic arrangement of classes and operations that solves a recurring object-oriented design problem. Each pattern gives the recurring shape a name so that one word can replace a paragraph of structural explanation.

sections.heading: Analogy
sections.body: Engineers reuse proven house designs for doors, windows, stairs, and layouts. Developers reuse design patterns for common coding problems the same way — pick a shape that already works, adapt it, ship it.

sections.heading: Where these definitions come from
sections.body: The intent, problem, solution, and idiomatic implementation for every pattern in this catalog are paraphrased from Nesteruk, D. (2022). Design Patterns in Modern C++20. Apress. and cross-checked against the original Gang of Four reference (Gamma et al., 1994). Every pattern detail page lists its sources explicitly.
sections.note: Nesteruk’s framing: a design pattern is a named, idiomatic arrangement of classes and operations that solves a recurring object-oriented design problem. The same problem keeps appearing because the underlying language facts (inheritance, ownership, virtual dispatch) keep producing the same shapes. Naming each shape turns a paragraph of structural explanation into one word a reviewer can look up.

keyTerms.definition: A named, structural arrangement that solves a recurring design problem.
The conventional way most experienced practitioners would write it.

summary: A design pattern is a named shape, not a piece of code. The same shape appears in many programs because the underlying language facts keep producing it.

title: Why design patterns matter
intro: Without patterns, code becomes repetitive, tightly coupled, and confusing. With patterns, code is easier to reuse, maintain, scale, and understand — and teams share a common vocabulary.

sections.heading: Industry evidence
sections.body: Studies between 2020 and 2024 quantify the gap between teams with and without disciplined design practice.
bullets: Poor software quality cost US firms an estimated $2.41 trillion in 2022 (CISQ).
Elite DevOps teams recover from failures 2,604× faster than low performers, an outcome tied to code-clarity and trunk-based discipline (DORA, 2023).
Classes implementing GoF design patterns show measurably lower change-proneness under maintenance than ad-hoc structures of comparable size (Ampatzoglou et al., 2020).
AI assistants ship code ~55% faster but shift comprehension load onto reviewers (GitHub, 2022, 2024).

sections.heading: Why this affects a beginner
sections.body: Reviewers and senior developers usually do not have time to explain every class. When the code carries a recognisable pattern name, the reviewer reads it in seconds instead of minutes — and you ship faster.

summary: Patterns are not academic vocabulary. They compress engineering decisions into one shared word that reviewers, mentors, and tools can all use.

options: Teams gain a shared vocabulary and code is easier to maintain

questions.rationale: Patterns make code easier to reuse/maintain and give teams a shared vocabulary; they are not about build speed or forced inheritance.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Foundations module content
questions.explanation: Patterns make code easier to reuse/maintain and give teams a shared vocabulary; they are not about build speed or forced inheritance.

options: It removes the need to compile the code
It guarantees the code has no bugs
It lets the reviewer grasp the design intent quickly without re-deriving it from scratch
It hides the code from other developers
questions.rationale: A recognised pattern name communicates intent quickly so reviewers understand the design without re-deriving it; it does not prove correctness.

title: Three main pattern categories
intro: Design patterns are commonly grouped into three beginner-friendly categories based on what the pattern is responsible for.

sections.heading: Creational
sections.body: Patterns that help create objects.
bullets: Singleton
Factory
Builder

sections.heading: Structural
sections.body: Patterns that organise and connect classes or objects.
bullets: Adapter
Decorator
Facade
Proxy

sections.heading: Behavioural
sections.body: Patterns that decide how objects communicate and choose behavior.
bullets: Observer
Strategy
State
Command

sections.heading: A fourth bucket — Idioms
sections.body: Modern C++ adds idioms that are not strictly GoF but appear often enough to be detectable: Method Chaining, Repository, and PIMPL. This site treats them as a fourth category alongside the three above.

sections.heading: Why the catalog is Gang-of-Four anchored
sections.body: The catalog is anchored on the four "Gang of Four" patterns first codified by Gamma, Helm, Johnson, and Vlissides in 1994. Their book named the recurring object-oriented arrangements that working engineers kept reinventing - Singleton, Factory, Adapter, Strategy and the rest - and gave each shape a vocabulary item that survives across languages. Nesteruk 2022 modernises those same patterns for C++20 (smart pointers, deduction, ranges) without changing what the patterns are. We anchor on GoF because the names are the lingua franca every reviewer already knows, and because the structural fingerprints (virtual interfaces, ownership handles, self-returning setters) translate cleanly into the token-level checks our analyser runs. A handful of non-GoF entries (Method Chaining, Repository, PIMPL) sit alongside them because the CodiNeo thesis explicitly lists them as detection targets.

summary: Creational makes objects. Structural connects them. Behavioural decides how they talk. Idioms are common shapes the catalog also detects.

questions.question: What are the three main GoF pattern categories described in this module?

questions.rationale: The three GoF categories are Creational, Structural, and Behavioural; the module adds a fourth bucket of C++ idioms.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Foundations module content
Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns, ch. 1
questions.explanation: The three GoF categories are Creational, Structural, and Behavioural; the module adds a fourth bucket of C++ idioms.

questions.question: On what basis are patterns grouped into those categories?
options: By how many lines of code they need
By the year they were invented
By what the pattern is responsible for — creating objects, organising them, or coordinating behaviour
By the author’s last name
questions.rationale: Categories reflect responsibility: object creation (Creational), structure/composition (Structural), and communication/behaviour (Behavioural).

title: OOP foundations
intro: Design patterns are built on object-oriented programming. Six concepts cover most of what you need before reading the catalog.

sections.heading: The six concepts
bullets: Class: a blueprint.
Object: an instance of a class.
Inheritance: reusing behavior.
Polymorphism: one action, many forms.
Encapsulation: hiding internal details.
Abstraction: simplifying complexity.

sections.heading: Example in C++

keyTerms.definition: The blueprint that describes how objects of a type are built.
A concrete instance of a class — what actually exists at runtime.
A single action that behaves differently per actual object type.

summary: Classes blueprint; objects instantiate; inheritance reuses; polymorphism varies behaviour; encapsulation hides; abstraction simplifies.

questions.question: Which OOP concept means bundling data with the operations that act on it and restricting direct outside access?
options: Encapsulation

questions.rationale: Encapsulation bundles state with behaviour and limits outside access to internals; the others are unrelated.

questions.explanation: Encapsulation bundles state with behaviour and limits outside access to internals; the others are unrelated.

questions.question: Why is polymorphism foundational to most design patterns?

It removes the need for classes
It lets code call a shared interface while different concrete types supply the behaviour
questions.rationale: Polymorphism lets callers depend on an abstraction while concrete types vary the behaviour — the basis of Strategy, Factory, Bridge, and more.

title: Program to an interface
intro: "Program to an interface, not an implementation" — instead of depending on one exact class, depend on shared behaviour or abstraction. The system becomes easier to change, expand, and maintain.

sections.heading: What this means in practice
sections.body: Take a list of payment processors. A bad design hard-codes one implementation (PayPalProcessor) into every caller. A pattern-aware design defines an interface (PaymentProcessor) and routes through it, so swapping PayPal for Stripe touches one factory, not every caller.

sections.heading: Where this appears in patterns
sections.body: Strategy, Factory, Adapter, and Bridge all rest on this principle. They give the abstraction a name and a recognisable shape.

summary: Depend on what something does, not who is doing it. Patterns formalise this principle into named shapes.

questions.question: The principle taught in this module is “program to an ___.”
options: interface, not an implementation
integer, not a string
inline function, not a macro
index, not a pointer
questions.rationale: The principle is “program to an interface, not an implementation” — depend on abstractions, not concrete classes.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Foundations module content
questions.explanation: The principle is “program to an interface, not an implementation” — depend on abstractions, not concrete classes.

questions.question: Why does depending on an abstraction instead of a concrete class make a system easier to change?
options: It deletes all the concrete classes
It forces every class to be final
It makes the program compile without linking
New or swapped implementations can be used without modifying the callers that depend only on the abstraction
questions.rationale: Callers that depend on an abstraction are insulated from concrete changes, so implementations can be added or swapped without editing them.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Foundations module content
questions.explanation: Callers that depend on an abstraction are insulated from concrete changes, so implementations can be added or swapped without editing them.

options: The exact PayPalProcessor class, but copied into each caller

questions.rationale: Depending on a shared PaymentProcessor abstraction lets any concrete processor be substituted without changing the callers.

title: Understanding software structure
intro: Source code is more than plain text. A system can analyse class relationships, inheritance chains, dependencies, and communication flow — and patterns appear as recognisable shapes in that structure.

sections.heading: The AST
sections.body: AST means Abstract Syntax Tree. It represents code as a tree-like structure so software tools can analyse syntax and structure without re-reading raw text. Most static analysis (including this site's pattern detector) operates on ASTs.

sections.heading: How CodiNeo uses it
sections.body: CodiNeo builds a parse tree from the user's C++ source, mirrors it into a virtual structural copy, and runs pattern checks against the virtual tree. The original parse tree is never mutated — so every detection result can be traced back to specific lines in the user's source.

keyTerms.definition: Abstract Syntax Tree — the tree-shaped representation of parsed source code.
Code analysis that reads the source without running it.

summary: Code is a tree, not a string. Patterns are shapes in that tree, and tools detect them by walking the tree, not by reading text.

seeAlso.label: Each pattern has a structural rule

questions.question: What does AST stand for in this module?
options: Abstract Syntax Tree

questions.rationale: AST = Abstract Syntax Tree, a tree representation of code structure that tools analyse.

Because the compiler renames every class
Because class relationships, inheritance, and call flow form recognisable structural arrangements a tool can analyse
Because patterns only exist in documentation
questions.rationale: Patterns manifest as structural arrangements (relationships, inheritance, call flow) that a tool can analyse from a parse tree, independent of comments.

title: Design patterns in real software
intro: Patterns are not academic. They appear in production systems written by working engineers across many industries.

sections.heading: Where they show up
bullets: Game engines may use Singleton or Observer.
UI frameworks may use MVC and Observer.
Databases may use Singleton.
Compilers may use Visitor.
Operating systems may use Command.

sections.heading: Why this matters to a beginner
sections.body: Recognising patterns in production code is one of the fastest ways to read a codebase you did not write. New contributors often spend their first weeks inferring conventions; pattern literacy collapses that period.

summary: Patterns are everywhere you actually work. Learning their shapes is the fastest path to reading unfamiliar code. When documentation drifts away from the code (which it eventually does), the recognisable shape is what survives.

title: Common beginner mistakes
intro: Learning patterns is easier when you know what to avoid.

sections.heading: Four traps
bullets: Overusing patterns. Patterns should solve real problems, not be forced everywhere.
Copy-pasting without understanding. Learn why the pattern exists and when to use it.
Ignoring relationships. Patterns are about structure, communication, and architecture — not only syntax.
Making classes depend too heavily on each other. Loose connections are easier to maintain.

summary: Use patterns to solve problems you actually have. Loose coupling beats premature abstraction every time.

questions.question: Which is a common beginner mistake this module warns against?

Forcing a pattern where a simple solution would do (over-engineering)

title: Ambiguity is built in to design patterns
intro: Some patterns look almost identical at the code level. Builder and Method Chaining both use `return *this`. Adapter, Decorator, and Proxy all wrap a held member and forward calls. This is not a bug — it is a property of the patterns themselves.

sections.heading: When two patterns share a shape
bullets: Builder vs Method Chaining — both rely on `return *this`.
Adapter vs Decorator vs Proxy — all forward calls to a wrapped member.
Strategy concrete vs Decorator — both override a polymorphic method.

sections.heading: Why this is the honest answer
sections.body: When two patterns share the same structural shape, no automatic tool can pick a single winner from the code alone. The right response is to surface both candidates and tell the reader the class is structurally ambiguous between them. Picking a winner needs human context the code does not yet carry.

sections.heading: Analogy
sections.body: Two siblings can look almost the same in a photograph. You cannot decide who is older from the photo alone. You need extra information — a birthday, a story — that the photo does not contain.

summary: Ambiguity between two patterns is information, not failure. It tells you human judgement is required.

seeAlso.label: Each pattern has a structural rule

questions.question: What does this module say about some patterns sharing the same code shape?
options: It is a bug in the catalog that must be fixed
It is a built-in property — different patterns can share a structural shape
It never happens in practice
It only happens in Java
questions.rationale: Shared structure across patterns is an inherent property, not a defect — e.g. Builder and Method Chaining both use return *this.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Foundations module content
questions.explanation: Shared structure across patterns is an inherent property, not a defect — e.g. Builder and Method Chaining both use return *this.

options: Because tools cannot read C++
Because the patterns are secret
Because several patterns can share an identical structural shape, so structure alone is insufficient to decide intent

questions.rationale: When patterns share a shape, structure underdetermines intent, so a tool cannot always pick one winner from code alone.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Foundations module content
questions.explanation: When patterns share a shape, structure underdetermines intent, so a tool cannot always pick one winner from code alone.

questions.question: Your analyser finds code matching both Adapter and Decorator equally. Following the module, what is the right response?

questions.rationale: The honest response to a true structural tie is to surface the ambiguity, not to fake a confident single pick.

Because the classes must be merged

title: The connotative-definition rule
intro: The way to reduce ambiguity is to add context to the definition itself, not to guess. This works the same way connotative meaning works in language.

sections.heading: Connotation and denotation
sections.body: Lumalalim ang kahulugan (connotative): kapag nagdadagdag ka ng mga descriptions sa isang salita, mas espesipiko ang kahulugan. Mula sa "parent", kapag dinagdagan mo ng "female", nagiging "female parent" — mas tiyak na. Nabawasan ang sakop (denotative): habang dumarami ang descriptions, nababawasan ang bilang ng bagay o tao na pasok sa depinisyon. Mas marami ang pumapasok sa "parent" kaysa sa "female parent".

sections.heading: How CodiNeo uses this rule
sections.body: Each pattern is defined not by a single keyword but by a small SET of structural descriptions — token combos that, taken together, narrow what counts as that pattern. The analyser rejects bare keywords as basis and accepts only stdlib symbols or multi-token combos.
bullets: Connotation = adding descriptions to make meaning more specific.
A single keyword like `virtual` is too thin — many classes have it.
A combo like (virtual ~) or (override {) carries enough context to be a real signal.

summary: Add structural descriptions until ambiguity disappears. Single keywords are noise; multi-token combos and stdlib symbols are signal.

title: When two patterns look identical
intro: Module 9 said ambiguity is built in. This module is the resolving idea: when two patterns share the same class diagram, you tell them apart by INTENT, and intent shows up in the lexemes, the collaborators, and the call-site shape — never in the skeleton itself. This is the same vocabulary the analyser uses to break a near-tie.

sections.heading: Structure alone is ambiguous — three classic collisions
sections.body: A class diagram answers "what shape is this?" It does not answer "what is this FOR?" Several Gang-of-Four patterns deliberately share one shape, so the diagram cannot separate them:
bullets: Strategy vs State — both are a context holding a pointer to a polymorphic interface and delegating to it. Identical UML.
Adapter vs Decorator vs Proxy — all three own (or hold) one member of an interface type and forward calls to it. Same wrapping skeleton.
Builder vs plain method-chaining — both have setters that end in `return *this` so calls can be chained. Same fluent shape.
sections.note: If you only read the boxes and arrows, each pair above is the same picture. That is by design, not a defect in the catalog.

sections.heading: The resolving idea: intent lives in the lexemes
sections.body: Intent leaves fingerprints in the tokens. The analyser groups those tokens into lexeme categories (see pattern_catalog/lexeme_categories.json) and reads the COMBINATION, not any single keyword. A few concrete C++ cues:
bullets: Self-return `return *this` (the self_return category) → a fluent/chaining shape. To call it Builder rather than bare method-chaining, you also need a terminator that returns a *product* (a `build()` / `finalize()` that hands back the constructed object). No terminator ⇒ it is just chaining.

An owned member of an interface type that is forwarded to, PLUS an added responsibility around the forward (logging, a new method, behaviour layered on the call) → Decorator. A bare forward with no added behaviour, just translating one interface to another → plain Adapter.

A nested wrap at the call site — `new Loud(new Plain())` or `make_unique<Loud>(make_unique<Plain>())` → confident Decorator composition; the Adapter detector treats that exact nesting as a NEGATIVE (a "Decorator giveaway").

A held state object combined with a transition call that swaps it — `setState(...)` / `changeState(...)` / `this->state_ = ...` → State. If the policy object is set once by the CALLER and never self-swapped, that is Strategy.

sections.heading: Intent also shows in collaborators and call-site shape
bullets: Collaborators — what other types must be present for this to make sense? Decorator and the thing it decorates implement the SAME interface (so a decorator can wrap another decorator); an Adapter bridges TWO unrelated interfaces. Same skeleton, different cast of collaborators.

Call-site shape — how the object is built and used. Builder reads as a chain that ends in a terminator; Decorator reads as a constructor wrapping a constructor; State reads as a transition call flipping the held member mid-flight.
Negative signals — the absence of an expected token is evidence too. "Fluent setters but no terminator" argues against Builder; "a wrapped member but no forwarding call" argues against Decorator.
sections.note: This is the connotative-definition rule (Module 10) applied to disambiguation: add descriptions — lexeme combos, collaborators, call-site shape — until only one pattern still fits.

sections.heading: How an analyser scores a near-tie
sections.body: When two candidates share a shape, a tool should not bluff a confident pick. CodiNeo scores each candidate per LINE, then compares them. The mechanics, conceptually:
bullets: Each catalog pattern carries weighted signals — callsites, expected collaborators, global functions — plus negative signals with negative weights. Every regex hit adds (or, for negatives, subtracts) its catalog-authored weight on the line it fired.

Per line, the pattern wins that line only if its own signal weight beats the opposing weight (rival patterns' hits on that line PLUS this pattern's own negative-signal weight). Wins become "successes"; every non-blank in-scope line is a "trial".

The line score is the Wilson score-interval lower bound of successes/trials (a 95% lower bound, z = 1.96) — not the raw average. Wilson is deliberately conservative: a pattern that wins 3 of 3 lines does NOT get a perfect score, because three trials is thin evidence.

A candidate is "confident" only above ~0.85. If the top two scores sit within ~0.10 of each other, the verdict flips to "ambiguous" and BOTH candidates are surfaced — exactly the honest answer from Module 9, now produced by the math instead of a guess.
sections.note: So identical structure does not force a wrong confident pick. It surfaces as a near-tie, and a near-tie is reported as ambiguity for a human to resolve with the intent context the code does not carry.

keyTerms.definition: A meaningful token (or fixed combo of consecutive tokens) the analyser reads as a signal — e.g. `return *this`, `make_unique`, `override {`. Bare single keywords are rejected as too thin.
A regex over how the class is constructed and used at the call site (e.g. a nested `new X(new Y())` wrap, or chained fluent setters) — evidence of intent that the class body alone may not show.

Another type that must be present for the pattern to make sense (e.g. a Decorator and its component share one interface; an Adapter bridges two). The cast of collaborators separates same-shaped patterns.

A catalog signal with a NEGATIVE weight whose presence argues AGAINST a pattern (e.g. nested constructors count against Adapter because they are a Decorator giveaway). Absence of an expected token is evidence too.
When the top two candidate scores fall within the delta (~0.10), the analyser reports BOTH rather than bluffing a confident pick — the structural tie made explicit.
The 95% Wilson score-interval lower bound of per-line wins/trials (z = 1.96). A conservative score that refuses to be confident on thin evidence, so near-ties surface as ambiguous.

summary: Identical structure is resolved by intent, and intent lives in the lexemes, collaborators, and call-site shape — not the class diagram. A line-by-line Wilson score lets near-ties surface as "ambiguous" instead of a wrong confident pick.

title: Each pattern has a structural rule
intro: Each design pattern in this catalog comes with a "correct structure" section that lists the exact token combos the analyser requires.

sections.heading: How to read the rules
bullets: Read the must-have list as: at least one of these combos must appear in the class body.
Read the must-not-have list as: if any of these combos fires, the class is rejected for that pattern even if the rest matches.
These rules are language-level signals — keywords combined with their immediate neighbours, or stdlib symbols whose presence alone is structural evidence.

title: Patterns vary by team and codebase
intro: Two teams that both claim to use Builder can write code that looks different. CodiNeo does not pick one team's convention as universal truth — it standardises on language-level structure so detection stays consistent.

sections.heading: What this means
bullets: Same pattern name can mean different things in different orgs.
Standardising on language-level structure (not naming) is what enables tooling.
CodiNeo's detection feeds into automatic unit-test generation, so a stable rule matters.

sections.heading: How to live with the variation
sections.body: If your team has stricter conventions, layer them on top. The analyser answers the structural question. The conventions answer the cultural one.

summary: Standardise on language structure for tooling. Layer team conventions on top for culture.

title: Post-Foundations open questions
intro: Before you move into the pattern catalog, sit with the questions below. They are intentionally open-ended — there is no single right answer, but every working developer should have an opinion on each.

sections.heading: Four questions
bullets: Unit testing — when the analyser flags a class as Builder, what unit tests would you write to confirm it behaves like a Builder rather than a Method Chain?
Value to a company — how does a shared vocabulary of patterns reduce onboarding cost for new hires? Where does it fail?
Readability — when does naming a pattern in code (a comment, a class name) help, and when does it just lock in the wrong abstraction?
Ambiguity in your own code — pick one class you have written. If a tool tagged it as two patterns at once, would you push back, or would you accept the ambiguity?

sections.heading: Note

summary: Patterns are also a conversation. The interesting questions are organisational, not just structural.

title: Singleton
intro: One instance. One global access point. Always the same object.

sections.heading: What it is
sections.body: A class that hands out the same instance every time you ask for it. The first call creates it; later calls return the same one.

sections.heading: When to use it
sections.body: When the object holds shared state that must not duplicate: a configuration registry, a logger, a database connection pool.

sections.heading: Everyday example
sections.body: The phone in the kitchen. There is one. Anyone in the house picks it up; everyone gets the same line. Adding a second phone in the same line would not change anything - it is still the one phone.

sections.heading: The shape in code
sections.body: Make the constructor private. Expose a static accessor that lazily constructs and caches the instance. Modern C++ uses Meyer-style local statics so the construction is thread-safe by language guarantee.

sections.heading: Must-have structural rule
sections.body: The combination of a private constructor and a static accessor is structural evidence the class can have only one instance. The detector confirms with object_instantiation + static_storage_access categories.
bullets: Static accessor — A function that returns a reference to the single instance, declared static so callers do not need an instance to start.
Static local instance — The single instance is stored as a function-local static so construction is thread-safe by language rule.

summary: Following Singleton makes the code more readable because the moment a reader sees `Logger::instance()`, they know there is exactly one logger, full stop - no hunting for a second constructor call.

questions.question: In the kitchen-phone analogy from the module, what does Singleton ultimately provide?
options: A way to assemble one object part by part
A way to copy a configured object cheaply
Exactly one shared object reached through a single access point
A family of matching objects created together
questions.rationale: Like the one kitchen phone everyone shares, Singleton yields exactly one instance reached through a single access point. The distractors describe Builder, Prototype, and Abstract Factory.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Creational module content
Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: Like the one kitchen phone everyone shares, Singleton yields exactly one instance reached through a single access point. The distractors describe Builder, Prototype, and Abstract Factory.

questions.question: Why does hiding the constructor matter for a Singleton, and how is it different from simply storing an object in a global variable?
options: Because hiding the constructor stops outside code from constructing extra instances, whereas a plain global still lets anyone call the constructor again
Because a global variable cannot be accessed from other files
Because Singletons are forbidden from using static storage
Because hiding the constructor makes the object copyable by default
questions.rationale: A non-public constructor is what enforces uniqueness; a plain global object does not prevent additional construction, so the single-instance invariant is not guaranteed.
sourceReferences: Nesteruk (2022), Design Patterns in Modern C++
CodiNeo Creational module content
questions.explanation: A non-public constructor is what enforces uniqueness; a plain global object does not prevent additional construction, so the single-instance invariant is not guaranteed.

questions.question: A game engine needs one audio mixer: created the first time sound plays, then reused by every system that emits a sound, with no second mixer ever existing. Which approach matches?
options: Prototype
Builder
Abstract Factory
Singleton
questions.rationale: One lazily-created object shared everywhere with no duplicates is the Singleton use case; the distractors solve copying, stepwise construction, and family creation.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Creational module content
Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: One lazily-created object shared everywhere with no duplicates is the Singleton use case; the distractors solve copying, stepwise construction, and family creation.

questions.question: Examine:  class Clock { Clock(){} public: static Clock& get(){ static Clock c; return c; } long now(); };  Which pattern does this structure indicate?
options: Factory Method
Singleton (Meyers function-local static)
Prototype
Method Chaining
questions.rationale: A non-public constructor plus a static accessor returning a single function-local static is the Meyers Singleton idiom; nothing here copies, chains, or chooses a product.
sourceReferences: Nesteruk (2022), Design Patterns in Modern C++
questions.explanation: A non-public constructor plus a static accessor returning a single function-local static is the Meyers Singleton idiom; nothing here copies, chains, or chooses a product.

questions.question: A class offers a static current() accessor but its copy constructor is still public, so callers can write Settings s2 = Settings::current();. Does this faithfully implement Singleton?
options: Yes — a static accessor is all that is required
Yes — copying is encouraged so each caller gets its own settings
No — a public copy constructor lets callers duplicate the instance, breaking the single-instance guarantee
No — Singletons must be allocated with new
questions.rationale: Uniqueness must be protected against copying too; a public copy constructor lets callers clone the instance, violating the invariant regardless of the accessor.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
Nesteruk (2022), Design Patterns in Modern C++
questions.explanation: Uniqueness must be protected against copying too; a public copy constructor lets callers clone the instance, violating the invariant regardless of the accessor.

title: Factory Method
intro: Ask a helper to make the right object. You do not pick the subclass.

sections.heading: What it is
sections.body: A helper that decides which concrete type to create. The caller asks for the abstract product; the factory hands back a concrete one.

sections.heading: When to use it
sections.body: When the calling code should not have to pick between several similar subclasses on its own. You ask for a Shape; the factory hands back a Circle or a Square.

sections.heading: Everyday example
sections.body: A vending machine. You press the button for soda. The machine decides whether to give you a bottle or a can. You do not pick the brand of plastic.

sections.heading: The shape in code
sections.body: Replace direct construction with a virtual factory method. Subclasses override the factory to return their own collaborator. The calling code keeps depending on the abstract interface.

sections.heading: Must-have structural rule
sections.body: A factory is identified by the act of creating-and-returning, not by the method being named create() or make(). Token combos like "return new" or a bare "std::make_unique" are language-level evidence that does not depend on what the developer chose to call the method.
bullets: Object instantiation in return position — A factory hands back a freshly created object. The combo "return new" is the most direct way to express that without naming the method.
Or a stdlib instantiation symbol — Modern factories return smart pointers. The presence of std::make_unique (or std::make_shared) is enough on its own.

summary: Following Factory Method makes the code more readable because the calling site asks for an abstract product and never names a concrete subclass - reviewers stop scanning for which subtype was hardcoded where.

questions.question: In Factory Method, which participant decides which concrete product is actually created?
options: The client code, by naming the concrete class directly
A subclass that overrides the creation method
A global registry shared by all callers
The destructor of the base product
questions.rationale: The base creator declares a creation method; a subclass overrides it to choose the concrete product. The other options bypass the subclass-driven choice that defines the pattern.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Creational module content
Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: The base creator declares a creation method; a subclass overrides it to choose the concrete product. The other options bypass the subclass-driven choice that defines the pattern.

questions.question: How does Factory Method differ from a factory that produces a whole matching set of related objects?
options: It cannot use inheritance at all
It always returns a single shared instance
It is identical — both create families
It defers the choice of one product to a subclass override, rather than creating a coordinated family of products
questions.rationale: Factory Method picks one product through a subclass-overridden method; producing a coordinated family of related products is Abstract Factory.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Creational module content
Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: Factory Method picks one product through a subclass-overridden method; producing a coordinated family of related products is Abstract Factory.

questions.question: A report engine runs an identical generate-and-export sequence, but each report kind must construct its own exporter object. The base class wants to stay unaware of which concrete exporter is used. Which approach fits?
options: Singleton
Prototype
Factory Method
Method Chaining
questions.rationale: A fixed flow whose single sub-object is chosen by subclasses through an overridable creation method is Factory Method; the distractors solve single-instance, copying, and fluent configuration.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: A fixed flow whose single sub-object is chosen by subclasses through an overridable creation method is Factory Method; the distractors solve single-instance, copying, and fluent configuration.

questions.question: Examine:  struct Parser { virtual Token* lex() = 0; void run(){ Token* t = lex(); /* ... */ } };  struct JsonParser : Parser { Token* lex() override { return new JsonToken(); } };  Which pattern does this structure indicate?
options: Factory Method
Abstract Factory
Builder
Prototype
questions.rationale: A base class calls its own overridable creation method while a subclass implements it to return one concrete product — Factory Method. There is no family, copy, or stepwise build.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
Nesteruk (2022), Design Patterns in Modern C++
questions.explanation: A base class calls its own overridable creation method while a subclass implements it to return one concrete product — Factory Method. There is no family, copy, or stepwise build.

questions.question: A reviewer finds a base Importer class with a virtual openSource() that two subclasses override to return different concrete sources, used by a shared import() routine. Is labeling this Factory Method defensible?
options: No — it must use a string switch to qualify
Yes — a creation method overridden by subclasses to choose the concrete product is exactly Factory Method
No — it is only Factory Method if the class is named Factory
Yes — but only if it is also a Singleton
questions.rationale: Subclasses overriding a creation method to select the concrete product is the defining structure of Factory Method; the class name and instance count are irrelevant.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: Subclasses overriding a creation method to select the concrete product is the defining structure of Factory Method; the class name and instance count are irrelevant.

title: Builder
intro: Set parts one at a time, then ask for the finished object.

sections.heading: What it is
sections.body: A way to build a complex object piece by piece. You set each part on its own line, then call a final method that returns the finished thing.

sections.heading: When to use it
sections.body: When an object has many optional parts, or when calling its constructor with all the arguments would be hard to read.

sections.heading: Everyday example
sections.body: Ordering a custom pizza. You add cheese. Then you add pepperoni. Then olives. At the end you say done, and the cashier hands you the finished pizza.

sections.heading: The shape in code
sections.body: Move construction into a Builder. Each setter returns *this so calls chain. A terminal build() method returns the finished object. Optional knobs stay optional; required knobs are enforced by the builder.

sections.heading: Must-have structural rule
sections.body: Builder and Method Chaining look the same at this token level. The analyzer flags a class with "return *this" as both candidates at once and marks the result ambiguous. The reader picks the right one based on whether the class also has a separate finishing method that returns a finished product (Builder) or just keeps mutating (Method Chaining).
bullets: Self-return chain — The setter returns the same object so calls chain. Without this, it is just a normal class.

summary: Following Builder makes the code more readable because each optional knob lands on its own line with its own name, instead of being a nameless positional argument inside an eight-parameter constructor.

questions.question: In the custom-pizza analogy, what does the Builder ultimately do at the end of the steps?
options: Guarantees only one pizza ever exists
Copies an already-finished pizza
Produces a matching set of related pizzas
Returns the finished object after the parts have been set one at a time
questions.rationale: Builder assembles a complex object piece by piece and a terminal step returns the finished result, like saying "done" to get the completed pizza. The distractors are Singleton, Prototype, and Abstract Factory.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Creational module content
Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: Builder assembles a complex object piece by piece and a terminal step returns the finished result, like saying "done" to get the completed pizza. The distractors are Singleton, Prototype, and Abstract Factory.

questions.question: Why is Builder preferred over a constructor that takes a long list of arguments for a complex object?
options: Because constructors cannot allocate memory
Because naming each part through separate steps makes the construction readable and lets optional parts be omitted
Because Builder guarantees the object is a Singleton
Because Builder removes the need for the object to have any fields
questions.rationale: Builder replaces a hard-to-read multi-argument constructor with named steps, so optional knobs stay optional and the intent of each part is clear.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Creational module content
Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: Builder replaces a hard-to-read multi-argument constructor with named steps, so optional knobs stay optional and the intent of each part is clear.

questions.question: An HTTP request object has a required URL plus many optional pieces (headers, timeout, retries, body). The team wants required parts enforced and optionals set clearly, ending with one finished request. Which approach fits best?
options: Assemble it through ordered steps and a terminal finish call
Make the request a single shared global instance
Clone a pre-configured request each time
Provide one factory interface returning a family of related requests
questions.rationale: Stepwise assembly that enforces required parts and ends with a terminal build is Builder; the distractors describe Singleton, Prototype, and Abstract Factory.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Creational module content
Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: Stepwise assembly that enforces required parts and ends with a terminal build is Builder; the distractors describe Singleton, Prototype, and Abstract Factory.

questions.question: Examine:  struct HouseBuilder { HouseBuilder& walls(int n); HouseBuilder& roof(std::string t); House build(); };  used as  House h = HouseBuilder{}.walls(4).roof("tile").build();  Which pattern does the presence of the terminal build() most strongly indicate?
options: Method Chaining only
Prototype
Builder (stepwise construction ending in a terminal build)
Singleton
questions.rationale: Chained setters that culminate in a terminal build() returning a separate finished product point to Builder; pure Method Chaining configures the same object and lacks the distinct terminal build step.
sourceReferences: Nesteruk (2022), Design Patterns in Modern C++
CodiNeo Creational module content
questions.explanation: Chained setters that culminate in a terminal build() returning a separate finished product point to Builder; pure Method Chaining configures the same object and lacks the distinct terminal build step.

questions.question: A "builder" exposes setters that return *this but has no terminal step and no separate product — callers just use the builder object itself as the final result. Does this honor Builder’s intent?
options: Yes — any class with chained setters is a complete Builder
Yes — a terminal step is forbidden in modern C++
No — Builder must be a Singleton to be valid
No — without a terminal step that yields a distinct finished product, this is fluent Method Chaining rather than Builder
questions.rationale: Builder’s intent is to assemble and then hand back a separate finished object via a terminal step; chained setters with no terminal product are Method Chaining, the closely related but distinct idiom.
sourceReferences: Nesteruk (2022), Design Patterns in Modern C++
CodiNeo Creational module content
questions.explanation: Builder’s intent is to assemble and then hand back a separate finished object via a terminal step; chained setters with no terminal product are Method Chaining, the closely related but distinct idiom.

questions.explanation: A creating-level check should produce code that the analyzer can recognize as Builder.

title: Method Chaining
intro: Set many options on the same object in one line.

sections.heading: What it is
sections.body: A style where each method returns the same object so you can call several methods in a row, all chained together.

sections.heading: When to use it
sections.body: When you want a clean, readable way to set many options on one object without writing the variable name over and over.

sections.heading: Everyday example
sections.body: Picking filters in a settings screen. You tap dark mode, then large text, then no notifications. Each tap leaves you on the same screen so the next tap can follow right away.

sections.heading: The shape in code
sections.body: Have each setter return *this. Calls fluently chain on a single expression. Reads like a sentence: subject, verb, verb, verb.

sections.heading: Must-have structural rule
sections.body: Method Chaining is structurally identical to Builder at the token level: both rely on "return *this". The analyzer surfaces both as candidates and marks the class ambiguous. The reader resolves it by asking: is there a finishing method that produces a different product (Builder) or do all calls just mutate the same object (Method Chaining)?
bullets: Self-return chain — Each call leaves the caller pointed at the same instance, so the next call can hang off the previous one without naming the variable again.

summary: Following Method Chaining makes the code more readable because related configuration calls collapse into one fluent expression that reads top-to-bottom like a sentence, with no repeated variable name in between.

questions.question: What does the Method Chaining idiom let you do in a single expression?
options: Set several options on the same object one after another
Guarantee that only one instance of the object exists
Create a matching family of related objects
Clone a pre-configured object
questions.rationale: Method Chaining returns the same object from each call so options can be set in one fluent expression. The distractors are Singleton, Abstract Factory, and Prototype.
sourceReferences: Nesteruk (2022), Design Patterns in Modern C++
CodiNeo Creational module content
questions.explanation: Method Chaining returns the same object from each call so options can be set in one fluent expression. The distractors are Singleton, Abstract Factory, and Prototype.

questions.question: Method Chaining and Builder both rely on returning *this. What distinguishes Method Chaining from Builder?
options: Method Chaining must be a Singleton
Method Chaining clones the object on each call
Method Chaining configures the same object fluently and has no separate terminal step that hands back a distinct finished product
Method Chaining can only set one option total
questions.rationale: Both share the return-*this shape, but Method Chaining simply configures one object fluently, whereas Builder adds a terminal step yielding a separate finished product.
sourceReferences: Nesteruk (2022), Design Patterns in Modern C++
CodiNeo Creational module content
questions.explanation: Both share the return-*this shape, but Method Chaining simply configures one object fluently, whereas Builder adds a terminal step yielding a separate finished product.

questions.question: A developer wants to write log.level(Warn).tag("net").color(true); on one line, mutating the same logger and reading like a sentence, with no separate finished object produced. Which technique enables this?
options: Make the logger a Singleton
Have each setter return a reference to the same logger so calls chain
Return a fresh copy of the logger from each setter
Provide one interface that creates a family of loggers
questions.rationale: Each setter returning a reference to the same object enables the fluent chain on one logger; the distractors prevent chaining or solve unrelated problems.
sourceReferences: Nesteruk (2022), Design Patterns in Modern C++
questions.explanation: Each setter returning a reference to the same object enables the fluent chain on one logger; the distractors prevent chaining or solve unrelated problems.

questions.question: Examine:  struct Style { Style& bold(bool b){ b_=b; return *this; } Style& size(int s){ s_=s; return *this; } };  with no terminal method returning a different type. Which idiom does this structure indicate?
options: Factory Method
Prototype
Abstract Factory
Method Chaining (a fluent interface)
questions.rationale: Mutating setters that return *this with no terminal step producing a distinct product form a fluent interface (Method Chaining). The absence of a build()-style terminal distinguishes it from Builder.
sourceReferences: Nesteruk (2022), Design Patterns in Modern C++
questions.explanation: Mutating setters that return *this with no terminal step producing a distinct product form a fluent interface (Method Chaining). The absence of a build()-style terminal distinguishes it from Builder.

questions.question: A "fluent" class declares its setters to return a brand-new copy of the object instead of *this. Does this implement method chaining correctly, and what is the effect?
options: No — each call mutates a different copy, so accumulated settings can be lost and the chain no longer configures one object
Yes — returning copies is required for chaining
Yes — it behaves identically to returning *this
No — chaining is impossible in C++ regardless
questions.rationale: Returning a fresh copy each call means each setter acts on a different object, so earlier settings may be discarded; correct chaining returns *this so the same object is configured.
sourceReferences: Nesteruk (2022), Design Patterns in Modern C++
questions.explanation: Returning a fresh copy each call means each setter acts on a different object, so earlier settings may be discarded; correct chaining returns *this so the same object is configured.

questions.explanation: A creating-level check should produce code that the analyzer can recognize as Method Chaining.

title: Adapter
intro: Wrap a class to make it look like another class.

sections.heading: What it is
sections.body: A wrapper class that owns an inner object with one shape and exposes a different shape to its callers, translating between them.

sections.heading: When to use it
sections.body: When you need to plug a third-party library into your codebase but its method names and types do not match what the rest of your code expects.

sections.heading: Everyday example
sections.body: A travel power adapter. The wall socket has one shape; your charger has another. The adapter sits between them and translates without either side knowing.

sections.heading: The shape in code
sections.body: Wrap the incompatible component in an Adapter that exposes the expected interface and forwards calls - possibly translating arguments - to the wrapped component.

sections.heading: Must-have structural rule
sections.body: Adapter, Proxy, and Decorator share the same wrapping signature at the token level. The detector emits all three as candidates and uses negative gates to disambiguate; the AI doc layer picks the role based on context.
bullets: Class wraps a member and forwards — The forwarding call (inner.method(...) or p->method(...)) is the structural fingerprint of every wrapping pattern.

summary: Following Adapter makes the code more readable because the rest of the codebase keeps speaking one shape - the translation between incompatible APIs is isolated to one wrapper class, not sprinkled across call sites.

questions.question: An Adapter primarily exists to ___.
options: let one existing interface be used through the different interface a caller expects
attach optional behaviour to an object at runtime
give a caller a controlled stand-in for a costly object
merge many small objects into a shared pool
questions.rationale: Adapter performs interface conversion so an existing component is usable through the expected interface. The distractors describe Decorator, Proxy, and Flyweight.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content
Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: Adapter performs interface conversion so an existing component is usable through the expected interface. The distractors describe Decorator, Proxy, and Flyweight.

questions.question: When an adapter receives a call, what does it actually do with the wrapped (adaptee) object?
options: It rewrites the adaptee so the two interfaces become identical
It forwards the call to the adaptee, translating names and argument types as needed
It stores the adaptee and discards every incoming call
It copies the adaptee once per caller to avoid sharing
questions.rationale: The adapter conforms to the expected interface and delegates each call to the unchanged adaptee, translating signatures. It never edits or duplicates the adaptee.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content
Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: The adapter conforms to the expected interface and delegates each call to the unchanged adaptee, translating signatures. It never edits or duplicates the adaptee.

questions.question: Your analytics layer is written against a Geocoder type with lookup(Address), but the only mapping vendor you can use ships a frozen SDK whose method is resolve(const char*, double&, double&). You may not edit the SDK. What design lets your layer call it unchanged?
options: A subsystem front that hides the SDK behind one easy method
A wrapper that adds caching to the SDK while keeping its interface
A tree of vendor objects treated uniformly
A class implementing Geocoder that translates each lookup into the SDK’s resolve call
questions.rationale: A class that presents the expected Geocoder interface while converting calls to the differently-shaped, unmodifiable SDK is an Adapter. The distractors are Facade, Decorator, and Composite.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content
Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: A class that presents the expected Geocoder interface while converting calls to the differently-shaped, unmodifiable SDK is an Adapter. The distractors are Facade, Decorator, and Composite.

options: Bridge, because the name says so and it holds another object
Decorator, because it changes the returned value
Adapter, because it implements the expected interface by converting calls to a differently-shaped existing API
Proxy, because it stands in for the probe
questions.rationale: Despite its name, ProbeBridge implements the expected TempSensor interface and converts a Kelvin reading into the expected Celsius call — interface conversion, i.e. Adapter. It does not vary two hierarchies (Bridge), add client-facing features (Decorator), or control access (Proxy).
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content
Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: Despite its name, ProbeBridge implements the expected TempSensor interface and converts a Kelvin reading into the expected Celsius call — interface conversion, i.e. Adapter. It does not vary two hierarchies (Bridge), add client-facing features (Decorator), or control access (Proxy).

questions.question: A class advertised as an Adapter implements the very same interface its caller already used and only records timing metrics before forwarding each call unchanged — no signatures are converted. Is the Adapter label correct?
options: Yes — any class that wraps another is an Adapter
Yes — forwarding calls is the essence of Adapter
No — because Adapters are forbidden from holding the wrapped object
No — with no interface conversion this adds behaviour over the same interface, which is Decorator/Proxy intent, not Adapter
questions.rationale: Adapter is defined by converting one interface into another. Keeping the same interface while adding work (metrics) is Decorator or Proxy intent; calling it Adapter misstates the intent.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content
Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: Adapter is defined by converting one interface into another. Keeping the same interface while adding work (metrics) is Decorator or Proxy intent; calling it Adapter misstates the intent.

questions.starterCode: // TODO: write a compact Adapter example.
questions.explanation: A creating-level check should produce code that the analyzer can recognize as Adapter.

title: Proxy
intro: Stand in front of an object. Decide what gets through.

sections.heading: What it is
sections.body: A class that mirrors another class' interface but sits between the caller and the real object, adding caching, access control, or remoting.

sections.heading: When to use it
sections.body: When the real object is expensive to construct, sensitive to expose, or located across a network boundary.

sections.heading: Everyday example
sections.body: A receptionist at an office. They take your message, decide whether the boss is in, and forward only the calls that matter.

sections.heading: The shape in code
sections.body: Substitute a Proxy with the same interface as the real subject. The Proxy decides when (and whether) to forward calls to the subject, adding caching, auth, or remoting along the way.

sections.heading: Must-have structural rule
sections.body: The wrapping signature alone is ambiguous; the access_control_caching category is what tilts the verdict toward Proxy.
bullets: Forwarding call — The proxy delegates to the real subject through a member access.
Access control or caching — A Proxy commonly serializes access through a mutex or guards a cache; this is what distinguishes it from a plain Adapter.

summary: Following Proxy makes the code more readable because cross-cutting concerns (cache, mutex, auth) live in one named class rather than being scattered across every call site that touches the real subject.

questions.question: The defining purpose of a Proxy is to ___.
options: compose objects into part-whole trees
decouple an abstraction from its implementation
provide a same-interface surrogate that controls access to a real object
convert one interface into another the caller expects
questions.rationale: Proxy supplies a stand-in that controls access to the real subject. The distractors are Composite, Bridge, and Adapter.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content
Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: Proxy supplies a stand-in that controls access to the real subject. The distractors are Composite, Bridge, and Adapter.

questions.question: Why must a proxy expose the same interface as the real subject it guards?
options: So it can be substituted for the subject transparently while it decides whether and when to forward calls
So it can offer the caller brand-new operations the subject lacks
So it can translate the subject into an unrelated interface
So that it can store all of the subject’s state internally
questions.rationale: Sharing the subject’s interface lets the proxy be used wherever the subject is, transparently controlling access (lazy load, auth, remoting). Adding new operations would be Decorator; translating would be Adapter.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content
Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: Sharing the subject’s interface lets the proxy be used wherever the subject is, transparently controlling access (lazy load, auth, remoting). Adding new operations would be Decorator; translating would be Adapter.

questions.question: A reporting screen references thousands of high-resolution attachments, but most are never opened. You want each attachment reference to behave exactly like the real document object yet defer the costly disk read until something actually calls render(). What design fits?
options: A wrapper that adds new export buttons to the document
A same-interface placeholder that loads the real document only on first use
A class converting the document into a printer interface
A shared pool that stores one document per file type
questions.rationale: A same-interface stand-in that postpones creating/loading the real object until it is used is a virtual Proxy. The distractors are Decorator, Adapter, and Flyweight.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content
Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: A same-interface stand-in that postpones creating/loading the real object until it is used is a virtual Proxy. The distractors are Decorator, Adapter, and Flyweight.

options: Decorator, because it does extra work before forwarding
Adapter, because it wraps another object
Composite, because it manages stored slots
Proxy, because it shares the subject’s interface and gates access without adding new client-facing operations
questions.rationale: GuardedVault keeps the same Vault interface and performs an access check before delegating, adding no new client-facing feature — a protection Proxy. Decorator would add a visible new responsibility; Adapter would convert the interface.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content
Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: GuardedVault keeps the same Vault interface and performs an access check before delegating, adding no new client-facing feature — a protection Proxy. Decorator would add a visible new responsibility; Adapter would convert the interface.

questions.question: A class wraps a RemoteLedger with an identical interface but does nothing except call through to it on every method — no lazy loading, no caching, no access checks, no remoting setup. Is “Proxy” a meaningful classification here?
options: Yes — any same-interface pass-through is a Proxy
Yes — forwarding alone fulfils Proxy’s intent
No — a Proxy must convert the subject’s interface, which this does not
No — without any access management (lazy load, caching, protection, remoting) it serves no proxy purpose
questions.rationale: Proxy is defined by some form of access control or management. A pure pass-through provides none, so the label is hollow. Proxy does not convert interfaces — that is Adapter.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content
Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: Proxy is defined by some form of access control or management. A pure pass-through provides none, so the label is hollow. Proxy does not convert interfaces — that is Adapter.

questions.explanation: A creating-level check should produce code that the analyzer can recognize as Proxy.

title: Decorator
intro: Wrap an object to add a behaviour. Stack as many wrappers as you like.

sections.heading: What it is
sections.body: A wrapper that owns an inner object of the same interface and adds extra behaviour around the forwarded call. Multiple decorators stack; the order of stacking determines the order of effects.

sections.heading: When to use it
sections.body: When you want to mix and match optional behaviours (logging, retry, caching, auth) without subclassing every combination.

sections.heading: Everyday example
sections.body: Sandwich toppings. The bread is the inner object. Each topping (lettuce, cheese, mustard) is a decorator that adds a layer without changing what is underneath.

sections.heading: The shape in code
sections.body: Wrap the object in a Decorator with the same interface. The Decorator forwards calls to the inner object and adds behaviour around the call. Decorators stack: each layer is itself decoratable.

sections.heading: Must-have structural rule
sections.body: Decorator differs from Adapter and Proxy by using interface_polymorphism: the wrapper itself is virtual so it can be stacked.
bullets: Same-interface forwarding — A Decorator implements the same virtual interface as its wrappee so the next layer can stack on top.

summary: Following Decorator makes the code more readable because optional behaviours stack as named wrappers (`Logging(Retry(Cached(inner)))`) instead of exploding into a combinatorial set of subclasses or boolean flag parameters.

questions.question: According to this module, the Decorator pattern primarily lets a program ___.
options: guarantee a single shared instance of a class
attach extra responsibilities to an individual object at runtime by wrapping it
simplify a complex subsystem behind one front-door class
represent a part-whole hierarchy as a tree
questions.rationale: Decorator dynamically adds responsibilities to an object through wrapping. The distractors are Singleton, Facade, and Composite.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content
Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: Decorator dynamically adds responsibilities to an object through wrapping. The distractors are Singleton, Facade, and Composite.

questions.question: What property of a decorator allows several of them to be layered on the same object?
options: Each decorator stores a single global instance the others reuse
Each decorator changes the wrapped object’s interface as it nests
Each decorator edits the base class so later layers can attach
Each decorator both implements the component interface and holds a component, so the wrapper is itself decoratable
questions.rationale: Because a decorator is itself a component that holds a component, decorators nest indefinitely while staying interchangeable with the undecorated object. They neither edit the base class nor change the interface.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content
Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: Because a decorator is itself a component that holds a component, decorators nest indefinitely while staying interchangeable with the undecorated object. They neither edit the base class nor change the interface.

questions.question: A message-sending service must let operators turn on any mix of retry, rate-limiting, and audit-logging per outgoing message at runtime, with each combination still usable wherever a plain sender is expected — and you want to avoid one subclass per combination. Which design fits?
options: A surrogate that defers creating the sender until first use
A class converting the sender into a third-party transport interface
A set of same-interface wrappers, each adding one behaviour around the forwarded send and stackable in any order
A simplified front that hides the sending subsystem behind one call
questions.rationale: Composable, interchangeable add-ons over a shared interface, combined at runtime, is Decorator. The distractors are Proxy, Adapter, and Facade.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content
Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: Composable, interchangeable add-ons over a shared interface, combined at runtime, is Decorator. The distractors are Proxy, Adapter, and Facade.

options: Proxy, because it wraps another Report
Composite, because it holds a Report
Adapter, because it changes the output
Decorator, because it shares the component interface and adds a visible new responsibility around the forwarded call, and can itself be wrapped
questions.rationale: WatermarkLayer keeps the Report interface, augments the wrapped object’s result with new client-facing content, and is itself a Report so it can stack — Decorator. Proxy would only control access; Adapter would convert the interface; Composite aggregates many children.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content
Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: WatermarkLayer keeps the Report interface, augments the wrapped object’s result with new client-facing content, and is itself a Report so it can stack — Decorator. Proxy would only control access; Adapter would convert the interface; Composite aggregates many children.

questions.question: A same-interface wrapper around a Connection only adds lazy opening of the socket and a permission check before delegating; it exposes no new operation or output to the caller. Is “Decorator” the most precise label?
options: No — lazy opening and access checks without any new client-facing behaviour are Proxy intent, not Decorator
Yes — every same-interface wrapper is a Decorator
Yes — lazy opening counts as a new responsibility, so Decorator
No — this can only be an Adapter
questions.rationale: Decorator specifically adds new client-facing responsibilities. Controlling access and deferring creation, with nothing new exposed, is Proxy intent. It is not interface conversion, so not Adapter.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content
Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: Decorator specifically adds new client-facing responsibilities. Controlling access and deferring creation, with nothing new exposed, is Proxy intent. It is not interface conversion, so not Adapter.

questions.explanation: A creating-level check should produce code that the analyzer can recognize as Decorator.

title: Strategy
intro: Swap the algorithm at runtime. The caller stays the same.

sections.heading: What it is
sections.body: A family of interchangeable algorithms with a shared interface. The host class delegates to a strategy reference and never names a concrete one.

sections.heading: When to use it
sections.body: When several algorithms could solve the same problem and you want to choose between them at runtime: sorting, compression, pricing, payment.

sections.heading: Everyday example
sections.body: Picking a route on a map app. The destination is fixed; "fastest", "shortest", "avoid tolls" are strategies. You swap the strategy without changing where you are going.

sections.heading: The shape in code
sections.body: Pull the variable behaviour into an abstract Strategy interface. Concrete strategies implement it. The host class holds a Strategy reference and delegates without caring which concrete one it has.

sections.heading: Must-have structural rule
sections.body: Strategy and State look similar because both inject a polymorphic collaborator. Strategy lacks state-transition methods; that absence is the differentiator.
bullets: Polymorphic interface — The host class depends on an abstract interface, not a concrete strategy.
Owned strategy — The host owns its strategy through a smart pointer so the concrete one can be swapped.

summary: Following Strategy makes the code more readable because the host class shrinks to one delegate call - the long if/else ladder that used to switch on a kind tag is replaced by the name of the chosen algorithm.

questions.question: A Strategy design groups several ___ behind one shared interface so the host can use any of them.
options: interchangeable algorithms
one-to-many notifications
state snapshots
grammar rules
questions.rationale: Strategy bundles interchangeable algorithms behind a common interface. The distractors describe Observer, Memento, and Interpreter.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Behavioural module content
Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: Strategy bundles interchangeable algorithms behind a common interface. The distractors describe Observer, Memento, and Interpreter.

questions.question: In a Strategy design, why does the host class keep a reference to the strategy interface rather than a concrete algorithm?
options: So the strategy can notify the host of changes
So that any concrete algorithm can be plugged in and delegated to without editing the host
So the host can inherit each algorithm in turn
So all algorithms share a single global instance
questions.rationale: Holding the interface lets the host delegate to any concrete algorithm interchangeably, so swapping behaviour never touches the host.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: Holding the interface lets the host delegate to any concrete algorithm interchangeably, so swapping behaviour never touches the host.

questions.question: A media exporter must encode video with H.264, VP9, or AV1, with the choice picked from a settings menu at runtime and the rest of the export pipeline untouched. Which approach fits?
options: Let an internal mode drive the encoder transitions
Notify a list of subscribers each time encoding finishes
Pull each encoder behind a shared interface and have the pipeline delegate to the one the caller selected
Wrap each encode request as a storable, undoable object
questions.rationale: Caller-selected, interchangeable algorithms behind one interface is Strategy. The distractors describe State, Observer, and Command.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: Caller-selected, interchangeable algorithms behind one interface is Strategy. The distractors describe State, Observer, and Command.

options: State — the rule changes the object’s mode
Template Method — a fixed skeleton with overridable steps
Command — the request is reified for undo
Strategy — the host delegates to an injected, interchangeable algorithm
questions.rationale: Invoice delegates the varying calculation to an injected TaxRule it never names concretely, with no state-transition methods — that is Strategy.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: Invoice delegates the varying calculation to an injected TaxRule it never names concretely, with no state-transition methods — that is Strategy.

questions.question: A class accepts a compression algorithm object through its constructor and delegates compression to it; the object never swaps itself out, and the caller decides which one to pass. Is labelling this Strategy defensible?
options: Yes — caller-chosen, interchangeable, delegated algorithms with no self-driven transitions is exactly Strategy
No — it must be State because behaviour is delegated to a polymorphic object
No — Strategy requires the host to inherit the algorithm
Yes — but only if there are at least three algorithms available
questions.rationale: Strategy is a caller-selected algorithm injected and delegated to; the absence of self-driven state transitions distinguishes it from State.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Behavioural module content
Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: Strategy is a caller-selected algorithm injected and delegated to; the absence of self-driven state transitions distinguishes it from State.

questions.starterCode: // TODO: write a compact Strategy Interface example.
