title: Observer
intro: When something changes, everyone who cares finds out.

sections.heading: What it is
sections.body: A subject that maintains a list of observers and notifies them on every change. Each observer decides what to do with the notification.

sections.heading: When to use it
sections.body: When the source of a change does not know all its consumers: UI views, log subscribers, real-time dashboards.

sections.heading: Everyday example
sections.body: A group chat. One person posts; everyone in the chat sees the message. The poster does not need to send it individually.

sections.heading: The shape in code
sections.body: A Subject keeps a list of Observers. State changes notify the list; observers update themselves. The Subject does not need to know what each Observer does with the notification.

summary: Following Observer makes the code more readable because the Subject expresses "something happened" once, and every reaction lives in its own named class - reviewers stop chasing a fan-out of direct method calls from the change site.

questions.question: The Observer pattern sets up a relationship in which ___.
options: a request travels through handlers until one accepts it
one subject automatically notifies many dependents when its state changes
two abstractions vary independently
an object snapshots its state for later restore
questions.rationale: Observer establishes one-to-many automatic notification. The distractors describe Chain of Responsibility, Bridge, and Memento.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Behavioural module content
Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: Observer establishes one-to-many automatic notification. The distractors describe Chain of Responsibility, Bridge, and Memento.

questions.question: How do dependents in an Observer design find out that the subject changed?
options: They periodically poll a shared global flag
They inherit the subject’s data members
The subject holds their references and calls each one’s update method when its state changes
They each rewrite the subject’s state directly
questions.rationale: The subject keeps a list of registered observers and calls their update method on change; observers neither poll nor inherit.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: The subject keeps a list of registered observers and calls their update method on change; observers neither poll nor inherit.

questions.question: When a sensor reading updates, a live graph, an audit log, and a threshold alarm must all react immediately, the sensor must not know their concrete types, and new reactors may be added later. Which approach fits?
options: Route every reaction through a central coordinator that decides who reacts to whom
Have the sensor maintain a list of dependents and notify each when its reading changes
Pass the reading through a series of handlers until one consumes it
Wrap the reading update as an undoable command object
questions.rationale: Loosely-coupled, extensible one-to-many notification triggered by the source is Observer. The distractors describe Mediator, Chain of Responsibility, and Command.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: Loosely-coupled, extensible one-to-many notification triggered by the source is Observer. The distractors describe Mediator, Chain of Responsibility, and Command.

options: Mediator — a hub coordinates mutual interaction
Visitor — operations are added via double dispatch
Observer — the subject broadcasts state changes to registered dependents
Iterator — it sequentially accesses elements
questions.rationale: Thermostat keeps a list of dependents and broadcasts each state change to all of them — the one-to-many notification of Observer.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: Thermostat keeps a list of dependents and broadcasts each state change to all of them — the one-to-many notification of Observer.

questions.question: Each dependent registers with a single source and is notified when that source changes; the dependents never coordinate one another’s mutual behaviour through a hub. Should this be classified as Observer or Mediator?
options: Mediator — any notification mechanism is Mediator
Command — it encapsulates requests as objects
State — the source changes behaviour with its state
Observer — it is one-way change notification from one source to many dependents, not centralised peer coordination
questions.rationale: One-way change notification from one subject to many dependents is Observer; Mediator would centralise two-way coordination among peers.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Behavioural module content
Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: One-way change notification from one subject to many dependents is Observer; Mediator would centralise two-way coordination among peers.

questions.starterCode: // TODO: write a compact Observer example.

title: Iterator
intro: Walk through a collection. The collection picks the order.

sections.heading: What it is
sections.body: An object that knows where you are in a collection and how to move to the next element.

sections.heading: When to use it
sections.body: Whenever you walk a collection. The C++ standard library is built on this pattern.

sections.heading: Everyday example
sections.body: A bookmark in a book. It knows the page you are on; turning the page moves the bookmark forward. The book does not change.

sections.heading: The shape in code
sections.body: Define an iterator that exposes begin / end / advance / dereference. The collection returns iterators; the consumer uses them. The collection can change its internal storage without breaking consumers.

summary: Following Iterator makes the code more readable because the traversal shape is the same for every collection - readers learn one idiom (`for (auto it = c.begin(); it != c.end(); ++it)`) and never have to relearn how to walk a new container.

questions.question: The Iterator pattern lets a client ___.
options: walk a collection’s elements one by one without seeing how the collection stores them
centralise communication between objects
restore an object to a previous state
wrap an action so it can be undone
questions.rationale: Iterator provides sequential access while hiding the collection’s representation. The distractors describe Mediator, Memento, and Command.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Behavioural module content
Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: Iterator provides sequential access while hiding the collection’s representation. The distractors describe Mediator, Memento, and Command.

questions.question: What is the benefit of moving the traversal cursor into a separate iterator object instead of exposing the collection’s internal array?
options: It forces the collection to become a single shared instance
It converts each element to a new type during the walk
It lets the collection notify dependents on every step
The collection can keep its storage private and even change it, while clients still traverse uniformly
questions.rationale: A separate cursor decouples traversal from representation, so the collection hides and can later change its internals while clients traverse uniformly.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: A separate cursor decouples traversal from representation, so the collection hides and can later change its internals while clients traverse uniformly.

questions.question: A library exposes a ring buffer and a skip list; users should loop over either with identical code and even run two independent passes over the same container at once, without knowing the storage. Which approach fits?
options: Give each container a hub that coordinates who reads next
Snapshot the container before each read so it can be rolled back
Hand out cursor objects that track position and expose a uniform advance/done interface
Add a visitor that defines a new operation per element type
questions.rationale: Uniform sequential access via position-tracking cursors that hide storage and allow concurrent passes is Iterator. The distractors describe Mediator, Memento, and Visitor.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: Uniform sequential access via position-tracking cursors that hide storage and allow concurrent passes is Iterator. The distractors describe Mediator, Memento, and Visitor.

options: Strategy — an interchangeable algorithm
State — behaviour driven by internal mode
Command — a reified request
Iterator — an object that tracks position and exposes done/value/advance
questions.rationale: An object that holds the traversal position and exposes done/value/advance, hiding the underlying sequence, is an Iterator.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: An object that holds the traversal position and exposes done/value/advance, hiding the underlying sequence, is an Iterator.

questions.question: A so-called traversal API requires callers to read the collection’s public head pointer and node links directly, chasing them manually to loop. Does this honour the Iterator pattern’s intent?
options: No — exposing the internal representation to callers defeats the encapsulation Iterator exists to provide
Yes — manual pointer chasing is the canonical Iterator
Yes — any loop over elements counts as Iterator
No — Iterator prohibits sequential access entirely
questions.rationale: Iterator exists precisely to hide the representation; forcing callers to use internal links breaks the encapsulation it is meant to preserve.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Behavioural module content
Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: Iterator exists precisely to hide the representation; forcing callers to use internal links breaks the encapsulation it is meant to preserve.

questions.starterCode: // TODO: write a compact Iterator example.
questions.explanation: A creating-level check should produce code that the analyzer can recognize as Iterator.

title: Command
intro: Wrap an action in an object. Replay it. Undo it. Queue it.

sections.heading: What it is
sections.body: A Command object captures the call: which method, which arguments, on which receiver. Calling execute() runs it; calling undo() reverses it.

sections.heading: When to use it
sections.body: When you need undo/redo, command queues, macros, or transactional actions.

sections.heading: Everyday example
sections.body: A coffee shop ticket. The barista does not memorise your order; the ticket records what to make and for whom. If you cancel, the ticket records that too.

sections.heading: The shape in code
sections.body: Wrap each action in a Command object with execute() and (optionally) undo() methods. Pass commands instead of calling functions directly.

summary: Following Command makes the code more readable because every action carries a name and a record - `UndoLastMove()` is more obvious than reverse-engineering an inverse-call sequence from history.

questions.question: The Command pattern turns ___.
options: a collection into a sequentially accessible sequence
a request into an object so it can be stored, queued, replayed, or undone
a grammar rule into an evaluable class
an object’s behaviour into a function of its internal state
questions.rationale: Command reifies a request as an object enabling storage, queuing, replay, and undo. The distractors describe Iterator, Interpreter, and State.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Behavioural module content
Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: Command reifies a request as an object enabling storage, queuing, replay, and undo. The distractors describe Iterator, Interpreter, and State.

questions.question: What does packaging an action as a Command object let the invoker do that calling the receiver directly does not?
options: Compile the program faster
Convert the receiver’s interface to another
Store, queue, replay, and reverse the action while staying unaware of the concrete receiver
Notify a list of dependents automatically
questions.rationale: A reified request can be stored, queued, replayed, and undone, and it decouples the invoker from the concrete receiver and operation.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: A reified request can be stored, queued, replayed, and undone, and it decouples the invoker from the concrete receiver and operation.

questions.question: A graphics editor must record each user action so the program can replay a recorded macro, queue scheduled actions, and reverse the most recent one. Which approach fits?
options: Wrap each action in an object exposing execute and undo, and keep them in a history list
Delegate each action to a strategy chosen by the user
Pass each action through a chain of handlers
Snapshot the whole document after every action
questions.rationale: Reifying each action as an execute/undo object held in a history is Command. The distractors describe Strategy, Chain of Responsibility, and Memento.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: Reifying each action as an execute/undo object held in a history is Command. The distractors describe Strategy, Chain of Responsibility, and Memento.

options: Memento — it snapshots state for restore
State — behaviour changes with internal mode
Strategy — an interchangeable algorithm
Command — requests reified with run/revert and recorded by an invoker
questions.rationale: Reified requests carrying run/revert, performed and recorded by an invoker that never names the receiver, is the Command pattern.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: Reified requests carrying run/revert, performed and recorded by an invoker that never names the receiver, is the Command pattern.

questions.question: A UI handler calls receiver methods directly inline and claims to support undo by re-reading the current widget values afterwards; there are no request objects. Does this implement Command?
options: No — Command requires each request to be a first-class object with execute/undo; direct inline calls are not Command
Yes — any feature offering undo is Command
Yes — calling the receiver directly is the standard Command form
No — Command forbids having any invoker at all
questions.rationale: Command’s essence is making each request a first-class object; calling the receiver directly without command objects is not the pattern.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Behavioural module content
Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: Command’s essence is making each request a first-class object; calling the receiver directly without command objects is not the pattern.

questions.starterCode: // TODO: write a compact Command example.
questions.explanation: A creating-level check should produce code that the analyzer can recognize as Command.

practicalExam.prompt: Write a small C++ class (or two) that demonstrates the Command pattern. The analyser passes you when the response tags include Command, even if other patterns also fire on the same class.

title: Composite
intro: A leaf and a tree look the same to whoever asks.

sections.heading: What it is
sections.body: A pattern where a container of Components is itself a Component. Operations on the container recurse into children automatically.

sections.heading: When to use it
sections.body: When your data is naturally tree-shaped: file systems, GUI widgets, math expressions, and you want the same operations on a node and a subtree.

sections.heading: Everyday example
sections.body: A folder on your computer. A folder can contain files or other folders. Asking "how big is this folder?" works the same whether it is a single file or a deep tree.

sections.heading: The shape in code
sections.body: Both the leaf and the container implement the same Component interface. The container stores Components and forwards operations to its children. Clients never branch on leaf-vs-container.

summary: Following Composite makes the code more readable because operations on a single node and operations on a whole subtree share the same call - readers stop tracking which arm of a leaf-vs-container if/else they are in.

questions.question: The Composite pattern is used to ___.
options: control access to an expensive object through a surrogate
share fine-grained objects to save memory
convert an existing interface into another
compose objects into part-whole trees so individual objects and whole groups are treated uniformly
questions.rationale: Composite builds part-whole trees in which leaves and containers are used the same way. The distractors are Proxy, Flyweight, and Adapter.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content
Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: Composite builds part-whole trees in which leaves and containers are used the same way. The distractors are Proxy, Flyweight, and Adapter.

questions.question: What makes it possible for a client to call the same operation on a single element and on a whole nested group without checking which it has?
options: The leaf type secretly inherits from the container type
Both the leaf and the container implement one shared component interface
A global registry records every node’s concrete type
Containers are forbidden from holding other containers
questions.rationale: A common component interface implemented by both leaf and container is what enables uniform treatment; the client never branches on node type. Recursion across sub-containers is exactly what Composite supports.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content
Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: A common component interface implemented by both leaf and container is what enables uniform treatment; the client never branches on node type. Recursion across sub-containers is exactly what Composite supports.

questions.question: A budgeting tool must answer total() for either a single line item or an envelope that contains line items and other envelopes nested to any depth, using one identical call regardless of which it is handed. Which design fits?
options: A shared component interface where containers recursively forward total() to their children
A wrapper that adds new behaviour to each line item
A surrogate that controls access to each envelope
A class converting envelopes into a reporting interface
questions.rationale: Uniform treatment of single items and arbitrarily nested groups via recursive aggregation through a shared interface is Composite. The distractors are Decorator, Proxy, and Adapter.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content
Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: Uniform treatment of single items and arbitrarily nested groups via recursive aggregation through a shared interface is Composite. The distractors are Decorator, Proxy, and Adapter.

options: Decorator, because Project wraps Tasks
Bridge, because two hierarchies are involved
Composite, because leaf and container share one interface and the container recursively aggregates its children
Flyweight, because Tasks are reused
questions.rationale: Chore (leaf) and Project (container) both implement Task, and Project recursively sums its children — the part-whole structure of Composite. There is no added responsibility (Decorator), no second varying hierarchy (Bridge), and no shared-state pooling (Flyweight).
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content
Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: Chore (leaf) and Project (container) both implement Task, and Project recursively sums its children — the part-whole structure of Composite. There is no added responsibility (Decorator), no second varying hierarchy (Bridge), and no shared-state pooling (Flyweight).

questions.question: A so-called Composite gives the container a children list and a render() method, but the leaf type exposes a wholly different method named paint() with no shared interface, so clients must test each node’s type before calling. Does this honour Composite’s intent?
options: Yes — leaves and containers are supposed to differ
Yes — having a tree is all Composite requires
No — Composite requires a shared component interface so leaf and container are used uniformly; forcing clients to type-check defeats it
No — Composite forbids containers from storing children
questions.rationale: Uniform treatment through a common interface is the whole point of Composite. If clients must branch on node type, the pattern’s benefit is lost. Containers storing children is required, not forbidden.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content
Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: Uniform treatment through a common interface is the whole point of Composite. If clients must branch on node type, the pattern’s benefit is lost. Containers storing children is required, not forbidden.

questions.explanation: A creating-level check should produce code that the analyzer can recognize as Composite.

practicalExam.prompt: Write a small C++ class (or two) that demonstrates the Composite pattern. The analyser passes you when the response tags include Composite, even if other patterns also fire on the same class.

title: Template Method
intro: A base class owns the algorithm. Subclasses fill in a few blanks.

sections.heading: What it is
sections.body: A base method that runs a fixed sequence of steps and calls virtual hooks at the variable points. Subclasses override the hooks, not the sequence.

sections.heading: When to use it
sections.body: When several subclasses share a high-level algorithm and differ only in specific steps.

sections.heading: Everyday example
sections.body: Recipes that share a cooking method but vary in one ingredient. The base recipe says "cook for 30 minutes, season, serve"; each variant fills in what to season with.

sections.heading: The shape in code
sections.body: The base class implements the algorithm and calls virtual hook methods at the variable steps. Subclasses override only the hooks; the algorithm shape stays in one place.

summary: Following Template Method makes the code more readable because the algorithm sits in one obvious place at the top of the base class - subclasses are easy to compare because they only differ in the named hook overrides.

questions.question: The Template Method pattern keeps an algorithm’s ___ fixed in a base method while letting subclasses supply certain steps.
options: list of observers
overall step sequence
state-transition table
request history
questions.rationale: Template Method fixes the overall step sequence in the base class and defers chosen steps to subclasses. The distractors belong to Observer, State, and Command.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Behavioural module content
Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: Template Method fixes the overall step sequence in the base class and defers chosen steps to subclasses. The distractors belong to Observer, State, and Command.

questions.question: In a Template Method design, what is fixed and what varies?
options: Everything is overridden by each subclass
The base method fixes the order of steps while subclasses override only the designated variable steps
Nothing in the algorithm can ever be overridden
The caller injects the entire algorithm as a separate object
questions.rationale: The invariant control flow lives in the base method; subclasses override only the marked variable steps, not the sequence.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: The invariant control flow lives in the base method; subclasses override only the marked variable steps, not the sequence.

questions.question: Three exporters share the flow open → gather → render → close and differ only in how they render, and you want that flow written exactly once. Which approach fits?
options: Inject the whole export algorithm as a swappable object the caller chooses
Put the fixed flow in a base method that calls an overridable render step subclasses implement
Pass the export request through a chain of handlers
Coordinate the exporters through a central hub
questions.rationale: A fixed skeleton in a base method calling an overridable step per subclass is Template Method; injecting the whole algorithm would be Strategy.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: A fixed skeleton in a base method calling an overridable step per subclass is Template Method; injecting the whole algorithm would be Strategy.

options: Strategy — the body is an injected algorithm object
State — build changes with internal mode
Template Method — a fixed sequence calling an overridable hook step
Command — build reifies a request
questions.rationale: A non-overridden base method fixing the order and calling an overridable hook (body) implemented by subclasses is Template Method.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: A non-overridden base method fixing the order and calling an overridable hook (body) implemented by subclasses is Template Method.

questions.question: An algorithm’s invariant steps live in a base method that invokes overridable hooks, and subclasses customise behaviour only by overriding those hooks via inheritance. Is this better called Template Method or Strategy?
options: Strategy — any variation in behaviour is Strategy
Observer — the hooks notify dependents
Composite — the steps form a tree
Template Method — variation comes from subclass overrides of fixed hook steps, not a composed swappable object
questions.rationale: Template Method varies behaviour through inheritance and subclass overrides of fixed hooks; Strategy varies it by composing a swappable algorithm object.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Behavioural module content
Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: Template Method varies behaviour through inheritance and subclass overrides of fixed hooks; Strategy varies it by composing a swappable algorithm object.

questions.prompt: Implement a small C++ example that demonstrates the Template Method pattern clearly enough for the Studio analyzer to tag it.
questions.starterCode: // TODO: write a compact Template Method example.
questions.explanation: A creating-level check should produce code that the analyzer can recognize as Template Method.

practicalExam.prompt: Write a small C++ class (or two) that demonstrates the Template Method pattern. The analyser passes you when the response tags include Template Method, even if other patterns also fire on the same class.

title: State
intro: Different mode, different rules. Same object.

sections.heading: What it is
sections.body: A pattern where each state of an object is its own class. The host delegates behaviour to its current state and switches state objects to change behaviour.

sections.heading: When to use it
sections.body: When an object has distinct modes that change which methods are valid or what they do: connection states, game phases, document workflow stages.

sections.heading: Everyday example
sections.body: Traffic lights. Red, yellow, green are states. The intersection behaves differently depending on which state is active, but it is the same intersection.

sections.heading: The shape in code
sections.body: Pull each state into its own class implementing a shared interface. The host holds a pointer to the current state object and delegates. Transitions swap the pointer.

summary: Following State makes the code more readable because each mode is a named class with its own valid transitions - scattered `if (state == ...)` branches collapse into one delegate call that means "do whatever the current state says."

questions.question: The State pattern lets an object ___.
options: change its behaviour when its internal state changes, as though its class changed
broadcast every change to a list of dependents
capture and later restore its state
pass a request along a chain of handlers
questions.rationale: State lets an object alter its behaviour as its internal state changes. The distractors describe Observer, Memento, and Chain of Responsibility.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Behavioural module content
Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: State lets an object alter its behaviour as its internal state changes. The distractors describe Observer, Memento, and Chain of Responsibility.

questions.question: How does the State pattern replace a sprawling switch on a status field?
options: By making the object a single shared instance
By copying the object once per status value
By representing each state as an object the host delegates to, with states able to switch the host to another state
By notifying observers whenever the status changes
questions.rationale: Each state becomes its own object encapsulating that mode’s behaviour and transitions; the host delegates to the current one, which can switch it.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: Each state becomes its own object encapsulating that mode’s behaviour and transitions; the host delegates to the current one, which can switch it.

questions.question: A vending machine behaves differently when idle, coin-inserted, or dispensing, and each event can move it into another mode with its own allowed actions. Which approach fits?
options: Let the caller pick the behaviour from a menu and inject it once
Give each mode its own object the machine delegates to, with each mode triggering the move to the next
Notify a list of subscribers each time the machine changes
Wrap each machine event as an undoable command
questions.rationale: Mode-dependent behaviour where the mode objects themselves drive transitions is State. The distractors describe Strategy, Observer, and Command.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: Mode-dependent behaviour where the mode objects themselves drive transitions is State. The distractors describe Strategy, Observer, and Command.

options: Strategy — an injected algorithm chosen by the caller
Observer — it notifies dependents
Command — a reified request
State — behaviour is delegated to a current-state object that performs the transition
questions.rationale: The host delegates to a current-state object that itself triggers the move to the next state — the hallmark of State, not caller-chosen Strategy.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: The host delegates to a current-state object that itself triggers the move to the next state — the hallmark of State, not caller-chosen Strategy.

questions.question: Behaviour is delegated to a polymorphic object, and that object itself decides, based on incoming events, to replace the host’s current behaviour object — driving transitions. Is this State or Strategy?
options: State — the behaviour objects drive transitions based on the host’s evolving state
Strategy — any delegation to a polymorphic object is Strategy
Observer — it notifies dependents of changes
Template Method — it fixes an algorithm skeleton
questions.rationale: When the delegated objects themselves drive transitions based on the host’s changing state, it is State; Strategy is caller-chosen and does not self-transition.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Behavioural module content
Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: When the delegated objects themselves drive transitions based on the host’s changing state, it is State; Strategy is caller-chosen and does not self-transition.

title: Repository
intro: Hide the database. Code talks to a clean interface.

sections.heading: What it is
sections.body: A facade over data storage. The rest of the program asks for entities; the repository handles where they come from.

sections.heading: When to use it
sections.body: In any layered application that talks to a persistent store. The thesis lists Repository among the patterns CodiNeo learners are expected to recognise.

sections.heading: Everyday example
sections.body: A library counter. You ask for a book; the librarian fetches it. You do not walk into the stacks yourself. Tomorrow they could move the books and your interface would not change.

sections.heading: The shape in code
sections.body: Define a Repository interface (findById, save, delete, query) and put the storage details behind it. Tests substitute an in-memory implementation; production swaps in the real one.

summary: Following Repository makes the code more readable because business logic reads as `users.findById(id)` instead of an inline SQL string - reviewers stop context-switching between domain language and storage syntax on every line.

questions.question: A Repository’s main job is to ___.
options: provide a collection-like interface that separates domain logic from how entities are stored
ensure only one database connection ever exists
attach optional behaviour to entities by wrapping them
convert a storage interface into a UI interface
questions.rationale: Repository gives domain code a clean, collection-like interface over persistence, hiding storage details. The distractors are Singleton, Decorator, and Adapter.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content
Fowler (2002), Patterns of Enterprise Application Architecture — Repository
Evans (2003), Domain-Driven Design
questions.explanation: Repository gives domain code a clean, collection-like interface over persistence, hiding storage details. The distractors are Singleton, Decorator, and Adapter.

questions.question: How should Repository be classified relative to the original Gang-of-Four catalog?
options: As a GoF structural pattern, alongside Adapter and Proxy
As a GoF creational pattern for building entities
As a C++ language idiom like PIMPL
As a supplemental enterprise / application-architecture pattern for data access, outside the GoF set
questions.rationale: Repository originates in enterprise/application architecture (Fowler; Evans) and is not part of the GoF catalog; it is an application-architecture data-access pattern, not a GoF structural pattern or a language idiom.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content
Fowler (2002), Patterns of Enterprise Application Architecture — Repository
Evans (2003), Domain-Driven Design
questions.explanation: Repository originates in enterprise/application architecture (Fowler; Evans) and is not part of the GoF catalog; it is an application-architecture data-access pattern, not a GoF structural pattern or a language idiom.

questions.question: Your service code must create, fetch, and delete Invoice entities identically whether they live in PostgreSQL in production or an in-memory map during tests, with the service code never mentioning SQL or connections. Which approach achieves this cleanly?
options: Make the database a single global instance the service reads directly
Define an Invoice-storage interface (findById, save, delete) with interchangeable backing implementations behind it
Wrap each Invoice in a behaviour-adding layer
Put a simplified front over the application’s user interface
questions.rationale: A domain-facing storage interface with swappable implementations (real DB vs in-memory) that keeps persistence details out of the domain is the Repository approach. The distractors are Singleton, Decorator, and Facade-over-UI.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content
Fowler (2002), Patterns of Enterprise Application Architecture — Repository
Evans (2003), Domain-Driven Design
questions.explanation: A domain-facing storage interface with swappable implementations (real DB vs in-memory) that keeps persistence details out of the domain is the Repository approach. The distractors are Singleton, Decorator, and Facade-over-UI.

options: A Repository: a domain-facing, collection-like interface that hides and lets you swap the persistence mechanism
An Adapter converting one storage interface into another
A Proxy controlling access to the database
A Bridge varying two independent hierarchies
questions.rationale: A domain-facing interface (findById/save) with interchangeable backing stores (SQL, in-memory) that conceals persistence is the Repository pattern — a non-GoF enterprise pattern. It is not interface conversion (Adapter), access control (Proxy), or two co-varying hierarchies (Bridge).
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content
Fowler (2002), Patterns of Enterprise Application Architecture — Repository
Evans (2003), Domain-Driven Design
questions.explanation: A domain-facing interface (findById/save) with interchangeable backing stores (SQL, in-memory) that conceals persistence is the Repository pattern — a non-GoF enterprise pattern. It is not interface conversion (Adapter), access control (Proxy), or two co-varying hierarchies (Bridge).

questions.question: A class named OrderRepository exposes methods that return raw result-set cursors and require each caller to open transactions and assemble SQL fragments. Does this faithfully realise the Repository idea?
options: Yes — the name OrderRepository makes it a Repository
Yes — a Repository is supposed to surface SQL and connections
No — leaking cursors, transactions, and SQL to callers breaks the domain/persistence separation the pattern exists to provide
No — a Repository may not return any data to callers
questions.rationale: Repository exists to insulate domain code from data-access mechanics. Exposing cursors, transactions, and SQL defeats that separation regardless of the class name. Returning entities is exactly what it should do — just not raw rows.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content
Fowler (2002), Patterns of Enterprise Application Architecture — Repository
Evans (2003), Domain-Driven Design
questions.explanation: Repository exists to insulate domain code from data-access mechanics. Exposing cursors, transactions, and SQL defeats that separation regardless of the class name. Returning entities is exactly what it should do — just not raw rows.

questions.question: Name the design idea a learner should create for this module.
questions.scenario: A learner must apply the Repository module to a small C++ design exercise.
questions.explanation: The expected answer is the module's core concept: Repository.

title: PIMPL (Pointer to Implementation)
intro: Hide the private members behind a pointer. The header stays stable.

sections.heading: What it is
sections.body: A C++ idiom where a class' private state lives in a forward-declared inner Impl struct, owned through a smart pointer.

sections.heading: When to use it
sections.body: When you need ABI stability across releases or when including the full private definition would pull in too many headers.

sections.heading: Everyday example
sections.body: A magician's box. The audience sees the outside (the public API). The trick mechanism inside (the Impl) can change between shows without anyone noticing.

sections.heading: The shape in code
sections.body: Move all private members into a forward-declared Impl struct. The public class holds a std::unique_ptr<Impl>. Callers see only the public methods; the Impl can change without touching the header.

summary: Following PIMPL makes the code more readable because the public header lists only what callers can do - readers no longer wade through private members and dependent #includes to find the API.

questions.question: What does the PIMPL idiom move out of a class’s public header?
options: Its private members, into a hidden implementation type reached through a pointer
Its public methods, into a base class
Its constructor, into a factory
Its return types, into templates
questions.rationale: PIMPL relocates private members into a hidden Impl type accessed via a pointer, keeping the header stable.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Idioms module content
Sutter (1999), Exceptional C++ — Compilation Firewalls (PIMPL)
questions.explanation: PIMPL relocates private members into a hidden Impl type accessed via a pointer, keeping the header stable.

questions.question: Why does PIMPL reduce client recompilation when private members change?
options: Because clients stop including any headers
Because PIMPL disables the preprocessor
Because the compiler caches everything forever
Because the private members live in the .cpp, so changing them does not change the header clients include
questions.rationale: With private state hidden in the implementation file, header-included clients are insulated from those changes and need not recompile.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Idioms module content
Sutter (1999), Exceptional C++ — Compilation Firewalls (PIMPL)
questions.explanation: With private state hidden in the implementation file, header-included clients are insulated from those changes and need not recompile.

questions.question: A heavily-included header keeps changing its private fields, forcing slow full rebuilds. Which technique stabilises the header so clients rarely recompile?
options: Make the class a Singleton
Add more public getters
Hide the private members behind a pointer to an implementation defined in the .cpp
Convert the class to a template
questions.rationale: Hiding private members behind an implementation pointer (PIMPL) stabilises the header and cuts client recompiles.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Idioms module content
Sutter (1999), Exceptional C++ — Compilation Firewalls (PIMPL)
questions.explanation: Hiding private members behind an implementation pointer (PIMPL) stabilises the header and cuts client recompiles.

questions.question: A header declares: class Widget { public: Widget(); ~Widget(); void draw(); private: struct Impl; std::unique_ptr<Impl> p; };  with Impl defined only in widget.cpp. Which technique is shown?
options: Bridge pattern
The PIMPL idiom (an opaque Impl owned via a pointer)
Flyweight pattern
Observer pattern
questions.rationale: A forward-declared Impl owned through a smart pointer, defined in the .cpp, is the PIMPL idiom.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Idioms module content
Nesteruk (2022), Design Patterns in Modern C++
questions.explanation: A forward-declared Impl owned through a smart pointer, defined in the .cpp, is the PIMPL idiom.

questions.question: A class holds one unique_ptr<Impl> whose sole job is hiding private members and stabilising the header — no abstraction varies. Is calling it “Bridge” accurate?
options: Yes — any pointer-to-implementation is Bridge
No — this is actually Adapter
Yes — Bridge and PIMPL are the same thing
No — a single fixed implementation used only to insulate the header is PIMPL; Bridge needs independently varying abstraction and implementation hierarchies
questions.rationale: One fixed Impl for header insulation is PIMPL, not Bridge; Bridge requires an abstraction and an independently varying implementation hierarchy.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Idioms module content
Nesteruk (2022), Design Patterns in Modern C++
questions.explanation: One fixed Impl for header insulation is PIMPL, not Bridge; Bridge requires an abstraction and an independently varying implementation hierarchy.

questions.starterCode: // TODO: write a compact Pimpl example.
questions.explanation: A creating-level check should produce code that the analyzer can recognize as Pimpl.

title: Prototype
intro: Copy an existing object instead of building a new one from nothing.

sections.heading: The shape in code
sections.body: Give the base class a virtual clone() that each concrete type implements to return a copy of itself. Callers clone through the base pointer and never name the concrete type.

summary: Reading clone() at the call site says "make me another of whatever this is" without a chain of if/else over concrete types - the polymorphism carries the intent.

questions.question: What is the core idea of the Prototype pattern?
options: Build the object up one part at a time
Keep exactly one shared instance
Create a new object by copying an existing one instead of building it from nothing
Produce a coordinated family of related objects
questions.rationale: Prototype makes new objects by copying an existing instance rather than constructing from scratch. The distractors describe Builder, Singleton, and Abstract Factory.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Creational module content
Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: Prototype makes new objects by copying an existing instance rather than constructing from scratch. The distractors describe Builder, Singleton, and Abstract Factory.

questions.question: Why does cloning rely on a virtual clone() rather than letting the caller write new ConcreteType(original)?
options: So the caller can copy an object through a base pointer without knowing or naming its concrete type
Because virtual methods are faster than constructors
Because Prototype objects may not have constructors
Because clone() guarantees the object becomes a Singleton
questions.rationale: A polymorphic clone() lets callers copy through the base interface without knowing the concrete type, which is the whole point of Prototype; naming the concrete type would defeat it.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Creational module content
Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: A polymorphic clone() lets callers copy through the base interface without knowing the concrete type, which is the whole point of Prototype; naming the concrete type would defeat it.

questions.question: A diagramming tool lets users press Ctrl+D to duplicate any selected element — a styled node, a connector, a group — at runtime, without the tool knowing each element’s concrete class. Which approach fits?
options: Singleton
Abstract Factory
Method Chaining
Prototype
questions.rationale: Duplicating a configured object polymorphically without naming its concrete type is Prototype via clone(); the distractors do not copy a configured instance.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: Duplicating a configured object polymorphically without naming its concrete type is Prototype via clone(); the distractors do not copy a configured instance.

questions.question: Examine:  struct Node { virtual std::unique_ptr<Node> clone() const = 0; };  struct TextNode : Node { std::unique_ptr<Node> clone() const override { return std::make_unique<TextNode>(*this); } };  Which pattern does this structure indicate?
options: Factory Method
Prototype
Builder
Abstract Factory
questions.rationale: A virtual clone() that each concrete type implements to copy-construct itself is the Prototype structure; it copies an existing object rather than choosing or assembling one.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
Nesteruk (2022), Design Patterns in Modern C++
questions.explanation: A virtual clone() that each concrete type implements to copy-construct itself is the Prototype structure; it copies an existing object rather than choosing or assembling one.

questions.question: A Prototype subclass owns a pointer to a heap buffer, and its clone() performs only a member-wise (shallow) copy. Is this a sound clone, and why?
options: Sound — shallow copies are always correct for Prototype
Sound only if the class is marked final
Unsound — both objects end up sharing the same buffer, so a deep copy is needed for a truly independent clone
Unsound — Prototype classes may not own pointers
questions.rationale: A shallow copy makes the clone and original share the same owned buffer, risking aliasing and double-free; an independent clone usually requires a deep copy of owned resources.
sourceReferences: Nesteruk (2022), Design Patterns in Modern C++
questions.explanation: A shallow copy makes the clone and original share the same owned buffer, risking aliasing and double-free; an independent clone usually requires a deep copy of owned resources.

title: Abstract Factory
intro: One factory that makes a whole matching set of objects.

sections.heading: The shape in code
sections.body: Declare a factory interface with one virtual create method per product. Each concrete factory builds one coherent family. Client code holds the factory interface and stays family-agnostic.

summary: Grouping the create methods on one interface makes the product family explicit - a reviewer sees at a glance which products are meant to vary together.

questions.question: What does an Abstract Factory provide through its interface?
options: A single shared instance reached globally
A set of methods that create a whole matching family of related objects
A copy of an existing configured object
A terminal step that returns one finished object
questions.rationale: Abstract Factory exposes one interface whose methods create a coherent family of related products. The distractors describe Singleton, Prototype, and Builder.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Creational module content
Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: Abstract Factory exposes one interface whose methods create a coherent family of related products. The distractors describe Singleton, Prototype, and Builder.

questions.question: How does Abstract Factory differ from a pattern where a subclass overrides one method to choose a single concrete product?
options: It is the same pattern under a different name
It only works when the products are Singletons
It defines an interface that creates a coordinated family of several related products, not just one product
It forbids the use of inheritance
questions.rationale: Abstract Factory creates a family of related products together; deferring one product’s creation to a subclass override is Factory Method. They are distinct.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Creational module content
Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: Abstract Factory creates a family of related products together; deferring one product’s creation to a subclass override is Factory Method. They are distinct.

questions.question: A document editor must render either a "Light" or "Dark" theme, and within a chosen theme the toolbar, scrollbar, and tooltip must all match — never a light scrollbar with a dark toolbar. Which approach fits?
options: Provide one interface that creates a matching family of related controls per theme
Keep a single global instance of the toolbar
Clone a pre-configured tooltip for each control
Assemble each control through ordered steps
questions.rationale: Guaranteeing a coherent, non-mixed family of related products per theme is Abstract Factory; the distractors describe Singleton, Prototype, and Builder.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
CodiNeo Creational module content
questions.explanation: Guaranteeing a coherent, non-mixed family of related products per theme is Abstract Factory; the distractors describe Singleton, Prototype, and Builder.

questions.question: Examine:  struct DbFactory { virtual Connection* connect() = 0; virtual Command* command() = 0; virtual Reader* reader() = 0; };  Which pattern is this interface part of?
options: Factory Method
Builder
Prototype
Abstract Factory
questions.rationale: One interface declaring several create methods for related products (Connection, Command, Reader) that a concrete factory implements as a coherent family is Abstract Factory; a single create method would be Factory Method.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
Nesteruk (2022), Design Patterns in Modern C++
questions.explanation: One interface declaring several create methods for related products (Connection, Command, Reader) that a concrete factory implements as a coherent family is Abstract Factory; a single create method would be Factory Method.

questions.question: A concrete MySqlFactory implements connect() returning a MySqlConnection but command() returns a PostgresCommand. Does this honor Abstract Factory’s intent?
options: Yes — any concrete return types are acceptable
No — a concrete factory must return products from one consistent family; mixing MySQL and Postgres breaks the family guarantee
Yes — provided both types compile together
No — Abstract Factory is limited to a single product method
questions.rationale: The value of Abstract Factory is that one concrete factory yields a matching family; returning a Postgres command from a MySQL factory violates that coherence guarantee.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: The value of Abstract Factory is that one concrete factory yields a matching family; returning a Postgres command from a MySQL factory violates that coherence guarantee.

title: Bridge
intro: Split "what it is" from "how it is done" so each can change alone.

sections.heading: The shape in code
sections.body: Hold the implementor through a pointer in the abstraction and forward operations to it. The abstraction hierarchy and the implementor hierarchy then evolve separately.

summary: The forwarding through renderer_-> makes the seam between abstraction and implementation visible in one line, instead of hiding it inside a deep inheritance tree.

questions.question: The intent of Bridge is to ___.
options: add responsibilities to an object by wrapping it
decouple an abstraction from its implementation so the two hierarchies can vary independently
provide a simplified interface to a subsystem
share intrinsic state across many objects
questions.rationale: Bridge separates an abstraction from its implementation so each side evolves on its own. The distractors are Decorator, Facade, and Flyweight.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content
Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: Bridge separates an abstraction from its implementation so each side evolves on its own. The distractors are Decorator, Facade, and Flyweight.

questions.question: Why does Bridge use composition between an abstraction and an implementor hierarchy instead of one big inheritance tree?
options: Because composing the two hierarchies lets each grow separately and avoids an N×M explosion of combined subclasses
Because composition makes the abstraction a Singleton
Because inheritance cannot use virtual functions
Because it merges both dimensions into a single class
questions.rationale: Bridge composes an abstraction with an independent implementor hierarchy, so adding a new abstraction or a new implementation does not require a subclass for every pairing — avoiding the combinatorial explosion inheritance would cause.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content
Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: Bridge composes an abstraction with an independent implementor hierarchy, so adding a new abstraction or a new implementation does not require a subclass for every pairing — avoiding the combinatorial explosion inheritance would cause.

questions.question: A reporting product has report kinds (summary, audit, forecast) that must each be emitted through several output engines (PDF, HTML, spreadsheet), and both the set of report kinds and the set of engines are expected to keep growing on their own schedules. Which design avoids one class per report-kind×engine pair?
options: A surrogate that defers building each report
An abstraction hierarchy of report kinds that holds and delegates to a separate, independently extensible engine hierarchy
A class converting one engine’s interface into another’s
A pool that shares one report instance per kind
questions.rationale: Two dimensions that vary independently, joined by composition so each grows without the other, is Bridge. The distractors are Proxy, Adapter, and Flyweight.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content
Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: Two dimensions that vary independently, joined by composition so each grows without the other, is Bridge. The distractors are Proxy, Adapter, and Flyweight.

options: PIMPL, because Alert holds a pointer to another type
Decorator, because Alert wraps a Channel
Composite, because alerts can be nested
Bridge, because an abstraction hierarchy (Alert) is composed with a separate, independently varying implementor hierarchy (Channel)
questions.rationale: Alert/CriticalAlert form an abstraction hierarchy that holds and delegates to an independent Channel implementor hierarchy; both vary independently — Bridge. It is not PIMPL (which hides one fixed implementation to stabilise a header, with no varying abstraction), not Decorator, and not Composite.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content
Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: Alert/CriticalAlert form an abstraction hierarchy that holds and delegates to an independent Channel implementor hierarchy; both vary independently — Bridge. It is not PIMPL (which hides one fixed implementation to stabilise a header, with no varying abstraction), not Decorator, and not Composite.

questions.question: A class holds a single std::unique_ptr<Impl> whose only role is to keep its private members out of the public header; there is exactly one Impl and no abstraction varies. A teammate labels this “Bridge.” Is that accurate?
options: Yes — any pointer to an implementation object is Bridge
Yes — Bridge and PIMPL are two names for the same construct
No — one fixed implementation used solely for header insulation is the PIMPL idiom; Bridge requires independently varying abstraction and implementation hierarchies
No — because Bridge cannot legally use a smart pointer
questions.rationale: A single, fixed implementation pointer used only to hide members is PIMPL, a C++ idiom. Bridge requires a genuinely varying abstraction hierarchy and an independently varying implementor hierarchy — absent here.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content
Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: A single, fixed implementation pointer used only to hide members is PIMPL, a C++ idiom. Bridge requires a genuinely varying abstraction hierarchy and an independently varying implementor hierarchy — absent here.

questions.explanation: A creating-level check should produce code that the analyzer can recognize as Bridge.

title: Facade
intro: One simple front door over a complicated back room.

sections.heading: The shape in code
sections.body: Introduce one class that owns the subsystem objects and exposes a few high-level methods that perform the choreography internally.

summary: start() reads as a single intention; the subsystem ordering it hides would otherwise be copy-pasted into every caller.

questions.question: A Facade is introduced in order to ___.
options: offer a single simplified interface over a larger or more complex subsystem
convert one existing interface into another
attach extra responsibilities to an object dynamically
guarantee a class has exactly one instance
questions.rationale: Facade provides a simplified front-door interface to a subsystem. The distractors are Adapter, Decorator, and Singleton.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content
Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: Facade provides a simplified front-door interface to a subsystem. The distractors are Adapter, Decorator, and Singleton.

questions.question: After a Facade is added, what happens to the underlying subsystem classes?
options: They remain reachable for advanced needs; the Facade only adds a convenient high-level entry point
They are deleted, since the Facade replaces them
Each of their interfaces is converted into the Facade’s interface
They are reduced to a single shared instance
questions.rationale: A Facade is additive: it simplifies common usage while leaving the subsystem classes accessible when finer control is required. It neither removes them nor converts their interfaces.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content
Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: A Facade is additive: it simplifies common usage while leaving the subsystem classes accessible when finer control is required. It neither removes them nor converts their interfaces.

questions.question: A checkout button should trigger one call that internally reserves stock, charges the card, books a courier, and emails a receipt, so callers never touch those four components directly and the components do not call back through it. Which design fits?
options: A surrogate that controls access to each of the four components
A wrapper that adds new behaviour to each component in a stack
A hub through which the four components route all their mutual messages
One class that owns the four components and exposes a single high-level method choreographing them one-way
questions.rationale: A single class exposing one high-level method that orchestrates several subsystem objects one-way is a Facade. The distractors are Proxy, Decorator, and Mediator (which would coordinate two-way peer communication).
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content
Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: A single class exposing one high-level method that orchestrates several subsystem objects one-way is a Facade. The distractors are Proxy, Decorator, and Mediator (which would coordinate two-way peer communication).

options: Mediator, because four components are involved
Adapter, because it converts the clip
Facade, because one class exposes a single high-level method that choreographs several subsystem objects one-way
Composite, because the steps form a tree
questions.rationale: MediaExport wraps a multi-step subsystem interaction behind one simple method (run), calling the components in sequence without them communicating back through it — a Facade. Mediator would govern two-way peer communication; Adapter converts interfaces; Composite builds part-whole trees.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content
Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: MediaExport wraps a multi-step subsystem interaction behind one simple method (run), calling the components in sequence without them communicating back through it — a Facade. Mediator would govern two-way peer communication; Adapter converts interfaces; Composite builds part-whole trees.

questions.question: A class billed as a Facade not only offers simple entry methods but also forces every subsystem object to route all of its mutual messages through it, coordinating their back-and-forth interactions. Is “Facade” still the most precise label?
options: Yes — Facade is responsible for coordinating communication
No — centralising mutual, two-way communication among peers is Mediator’s intent; Facade is strictly one-way simplification
Yes — Facade and Mediator describe the same role
No — this is actually an Adapter
questions.rationale: Once a class governs two-way communication among a set of peers, its intent matches Mediator. A Facade only simplifies one-way access to a subsystem; it does not mediate peer interactions.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content
Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: Once a class governs two-way communication among a set of peers, its intent matches Mediator. A Facade only simplifies one-way access to a subsystem; it does not mediate peer interactions.

title: Flyweight
intro: Share one copy of identical things instead of making thousands.

sections.heading: The shape in code
sections.body: Keep a pool keyed by the intrinsic state and hand out shared instances. Extrinsic state is passed in per call instead of stored.

summary: The pool_ lookup states the sharing policy in one place; readers see immediately that instances are interned, not duplicated.

questions.question: In this module, the main goal of the Flyweight pattern is to ___.
options: compose objects into part-whole trees
decouple an abstraction from its implementation
provide a controlled surrogate for a costly object
support very large numbers of similar objects efficiently by sharing their common state
questions.rationale: Flyweight reduces memory by sharing common (intrinsic) state across many fine-grained objects. The distractors are Composite, Bridge, and Proxy.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content
Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: Flyweight reduces memory by sharing common (intrinsic) state across many fine-grained objects. The distractors are Composite, Bridge, and Proxy.

questions.question: In a Flyweight design, where do intrinsic and extrinsic state respectively live?
options: Intrinsic state is shared and stored inside the flyweight; extrinsic state is context-dependent and supplied by the client at call time
Both intrinsic and extrinsic state are stored inside each shared flyweight
Both are passed in by the client on every call and never stored
Intrinsic state is per-client; extrinsic state is the shared part
questions.rationale: Intrinsic (shared) state lives in the flyweight; extrinsic (context) state must be supplied by the client per operation so it is not duplicated across the shared instances.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content
Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: Intrinsic (shared) state lives in the flyweight; extrinsic (context) state must be supplied by the client per operation so it is not duplicated across the shared instances.

questions.question: A tile-based game draws millions of map cells drawn from a handful of terrain kinds; each kind’s sprite and movement-cost repeat across cells, while each cell’s grid coordinates differ. How do you avoid storing the sprite data once per cell?
options: Build a tree of cells treated uniformly
Keep one shared object per terrain kind holding the repeating data, and pass each cell’s coordinates in at draw time
Wrap each cell in a behaviour-adding layer
Give each cell a surrogate that controls access
questions.rationale: Sharing the repeating intrinsic data per terrain kind while passing per-cell position (extrinsic) at call time is Flyweight. The distractors are Composite, Decorator, and Proxy.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content
Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: Sharing the repeating intrinsic data per terrain kind while passing per-cell position (extrinsic) at call time is Flyweight. The distractors are Composite, Decorator, and Proxy.

options: Prototype, because new particles are produced
Flyweight, because a pool hands out shared instances keyed by intrinsic state while per-call context (position, velocity) is passed in
Singleton, because the pool is shared
Proxy, because get() stands in for a particle
questions.rationale: ParticlePool returns shared instances keyed by intrinsic data (color/sprite) and the per-use position/velocity is passed to draw() as extrinsic state — Flyweight. It is not cloning (Prototype), a single global instance (Singleton), or access control (Proxy).
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content
Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: ParticlePool returns shared instances keyed by intrinsic data (color/sprite) and the per-use position/velocity is passed to draw() as extrinsic state — Flyweight. It is not cloning (Prototype), a single global instance (Singleton), or access control (Proxy).

questions.question: A design described as Flyweight stores each glyph’s on-screen x/y as a member field and creates a brand-new object for every character on the page, sharing nothing. Does this achieve Flyweight’s goal?
options: Yes — a Flyweight is supposed to store all state internally
Yes — one object per character is the intended outcome
No — keeping per-instance extrinsic state (position) and never sharing instances negates Flyweight; position should be extrinsic and instances shared
No — because Flyweight is not allowed to use a factory or pool
questions.rationale: Flyweight’s savings come from sharing instances and externalising context state. Storing each glyph’s position internally and creating one object per character defeats the purpose. A factory/pool is in fact a normal part of Flyweight.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content
Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: Flyweight’s savings come from sharing instances and externalising context state. Storing each glyph’s position internally and creating one object per character defeats the purpose. A factory/pool is in fact a normal part of Flyweight.

title: Chain of Responsibility
intro: Hand the request down the line until someone takes it.

sections.heading: The shape in code
sections.body: Give each handler a link to the next and a virtual handle(). A handler either processes the request or delegates to its successor.

summary: The next_->handle() forward makes the fallback path explicit, so a reviewer can follow who gets a shot at the request without a giant switch.

questions.question: In the Chain of Responsibility pattern, a request ___.
options: is snapshotted so it can be restored later
travels along a series of handlers until one of them handles it
is broadcast to every registered dependent at once
is evaluated as a tree of grammar rules
questions.rationale: Chain of Responsibility forwards a request through handlers until one handles it, decoupling sender from receiver. The distractors describe Memento, Observer, and Interpreter.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Behavioural module content
Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: Chain of Responsibility forwards a request through handlers until one handles it, decoupling sender from receiver. The distractors describe Memento, Observer, and Interpreter.

questions.question: What decision does each handler in the chain make when it receives a request?
options: Which dependents to notify
How to convert the request’s interface
Whether to handle the request itself or pass it on to its successor
Which single shared instance to return
questions.rationale: Each handler either handles the request or forwards it to the next handler in the chain.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: Each handler either handles the request or forwards it to the next handler in the chain.

questions.question: An expense claim should be tried by a team lead, then a department head, then finance, each either approving within its limit or passing it on, with the submitter unaware of who ultimately approves. Which approach fits?
options: Coordinate the approvers through a central hub that routes the claim
Link the approvers so each handles the claim within its limit or forwards it to the next
Notify all approvers simultaneously and let each react
Snapshot the claim before each approval step
questions.rationale: Linked handlers that each handle-or-forward while hiding the receiver from the sender is Chain of Responsibility. The distractors describe Mediator, Observer, and Memento.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: Linked handlers that each handle-or-forward while hiding the receiver from the sender is Chain of Responsibility. The distractors describe Mediator, Observer, and Memento.

options: Decorator — a wrapper that always augments and delegates
Composite — a part-whole tree
Observer — a subject notifying dependents
Chain of Responsibility — linked handlers that each handle or forward, possibly stopping the request
questions.rationale: Each handler holds a successor and chooses to consume (stop) or forward the request — Chain of Responsibility, distinct from a Decorator that always delegates.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: Each handler holds a successor and chooses to consume (stop) or forward the request — Chain of Responsibility, distinct from a Decorator that always delegates.

questions.question: Each link holds a reference to the next and either stops the request on a condition or calls the next, and the caller never learns which link acted. A teammate calls this Decorator. Is that defensible?
options: No — a link that may stop the request and hides the receiver from the sender is Chain of Responsibility, not a Decorator that always delegates while adding behaviour
Yes — any chain of wrappers holding a next reference is Decorator
Yes — because both forward to a successor
No — but only because Decorator requires inheritance
questions.rationale: Conditional handle-or-stop with sender/receiver decoupling is Chain of Responsibility; a Decorator always delegates while augmenting, so the label is wrong.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Behavioural module content
Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: Conditional handle-or-stop with sender/receiver decoupling is Chain of Responsibility; a Decorator always delegates while augmenting, so the label is wrong.

questions.starterCode: // TODO: write a compact Chain Of Responsibility example.
questions.explanation: A creating-level check should produce code that the analyzer can recognize as Chain Of Responsibility.

title: Mediator
intro: Components talk through one hub instead of to each other.

sections.heading: The shape in code
sections.body: Route all interaction through a mediator. Components notify the mediator, which decides who else needs to react.

summary: A single notify() entry point concentrates the interaction rules in one class, so reviewers stop tracing N-to-N call chains between components.

questions.question: According to this module, a Mediator object ___.
options: evaluates sentences of a small grammar
gives sequential access to a collection
captures an object’s state for restore
centralises and coordinates communication among a set of objects so they need not reference each other directly
questions.rationale: Mediator centralises inter-object communication so colleagues stay decoupled. The distractors describe Interpreter, Iterator, and Memento.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Behavioural module content
Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: Mediator centralises inter-object communication so colleagues stay decoupled. The distractors describe Interpreter, Iterator, and Memento.

questions.question: How does a Mediator reduce coupling among its colleague objects?
options: By merging all colleagues into a single class
By converting each colleague’s interface to a common one
Colleagues talk only to the mediator, which routes interactions, instead of holding references to one another
By making each colleague a single shared instance
questions.rationale: Colleagues communicate through the mediator rather than referencing each other, replacing tangled many-to-many links with one hub.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: Colleagues communicate through the mediator rather than referencing each other, replacing tangled many-to-many links with one hub.

questions.question: In a booking form, selecting a country must refilter the city list, toggle a postcode field, and enable the submit button; you want to avoid each control holding references to all the others. Which approach fits?
options: Route every control’s events through one coordinator that decides how the others react
Have each control notify a list of dependents and update themselves
Pass each event through a chain of handlers until one acts
Give each control an interchangeable algorithm chosen at runtime
questions.rationale: Centralising the controls’ mutual interactions in one coordinator is Mediator; one-way notification would be Observer.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: Centralising the controls’ mutual interactions in one coordinator is Mediator; one-way notification would be Observer.

options: Observer — a subject broadcasting to dependents
Mediator — a hub coordinating the mutual interaction of otherwise-decoupled peers
Facade — a simplified entry point to a subsystem
Visitor — operations added by double dispatch
questions.rationale: Peers (planes) that never reference each other and interact only through a central coordinator that governs their mutual behaviour is Mediator.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: Peers (planes) that never reference each other and interact only through a central coordinator that governs their mutual behaviour is Mediator.

questions.question: A hub receives events from peer objects and instructs other peers how to respond, governing their two-way interaction — not merely forwarding one-way change notifications and not just exposing a simpler entry point to a subsystem. Best classified as?
options: Observer — it is one-to-many change notification
Facade — it only simplifies access to a subsystem
Adapter — it converts incompatible interfaces
Mediator — it centralises and coordinates the peers’ mutual communication
questions.rationale: Two-way coordination of peers’ mutual interactions is Mediator; Observer is one-way notification and Facade is one-way subsystem simplification.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Behavioural module content
Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: Two-way coordination of peers’ mutual interactions is Mediator; Observer is one-way notification and Facade is one-way subsystem simplification.
