questions.starterCode: // TODO: write a compact Mediator example.
questions.explanation: A creating-level check should produce code that the analyzer can recognize as Mediator.

practicalExam.prompt: Write a small C++ class (or two) that demonstrates the Mediator pattern. The analyser passes you when the response tags include Mediator, even if other patterns also fire on the same class.

title: Visitor
intro: Add new operations to a hierarchy without touching its classes.

sections.heading: The shape in code
sections.body: Move the operation into a visitor with one visit() per element type. Elements expose accept(Visitor&) and call back the matching visit().

summary: Each new operation lives in its own visitor class, so a reviewer reads one cohesive file instead of a method scattered across every element type.

questions.question: The Visitor pattern lets you ___.
options: add new operations over an object structure without modifying the structure’s element classes
walk a collection while hiding its storage
centralise communication between objects
restore an object to a saved state
questions.rationale: Visitor adds operations over a structure without editing the element classes. The distractors describe Iterator, Mediator, and Memento.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Behavioural module content
Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: Visitor adds operations over a structure without editing the element classes. The distractors describe Iterator, Mediator, and Memento.

questions.question: What technique lets the correct operation run for each concrete element type in a Visitor design?
options: A global switch over an integer type tag
Reflection performed at link time
Double dispatch — each element’s accept calls back the visitor overload matching the element’s concrete type
Copying each element before operating on it
questions.rationale: Double dispatch (element.accept → the visitor’s type-specific visit) selects the right operation for each concrete element type; a new operation is just a new visitor.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: Double dispatch (element.accept → the visitor’s type-specific visit) selects the right operation for each concrete element type; a new operation is just a new visitor.

questions.question: A document model has paragraph, image, and table nodes; you must keep adding operations like word-count, export-to-HTML, and spell-check without editing each node class for every new operation. Which approach fits?
options: Give each node a current-state object that changes its behaviour
Move each operation into a separate visitor with one visit method per node type, with nodes calling back via accept
Pass each node through a chain of handlers
Coordinate the nodes through a central hub
questions.rationale: Adding many operations over a stable element hierarchy without editing the elements, via accept/visit double dispatch, is Visitor. The distractors describe State, Chain of Responsibility, and Mediator.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: Adding many operations over a stable element hierarchy without editing the elements, via accept/visit double dispatch, is Visitor. The distractors describe State, Chain of Responsibility, and Mediator.

options: Composite — a part-whole tree
Mediator — peers coordinated by a hub
Observer — a subject notifying dependents
Visitor — accept/visit double dispatch adding operations over an element hierarchy
questions.rationale: Elements exposing accept and calling back a type-specific operation (double dispatch) so operations live in the operation object is Visitor.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: Elements exposing accept and calling back a type-specific operation (double dispatch) so operations live in the operation object is Visitor.

questions.question: A team must add many unrelated operations over a node hierarchy whose set of node types almost never changes. Is Visitor an appropriate choice, and why?
options: Yes — Visitor suits a stable element set with frequently-added operations, isolating each operation in its own visitor (accepting that new element types would be costly)
No — Visitor is meant for adding new element types cheaply
No — Visitor can express only a single operation
Yes — because Visitor makes adding new node types trivial
questions.rationale: Visitor fits stable element hierarchies with many added operations; its known cost is that introducing a new element type forces edits to every visitor.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Behavioural module content
Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: Visitor fits stable element hierarchies with many added operations; its known cost is that introducing a new element type forces edits to every visitor.

questions.starterCode: // TODO: write a compact Visitor example.
questions.explanation: A creating-level check should produce code that the analyzer can recognize as Visitor.

practicalExam.prompt: Write a small C++ class (or two) that demonstrates the Visitor pattern. The analyser passes you when the response tags include Visitor, even if other patterns also fire on the same class.

title: Interpreter
intro: Turn each grammar rule into a class that knows how to evaluate itself.

sections.heading: The shape in code
sections.body: Model each grammar rule as a class implementing a virtual interpret(Context&). Composite expressions interpret their children to produce a result.

summary: interpret(ctx) on each node makes the grammar map one-to-one onto classes, so the language structure is readable in the type hierarchy.

questions.question: The Interpreter pattern represents a small language by ___.
options: snapshotting the parser’s state for undo
defining a class for each grammar rule and using them to evaluate sentences
broadcasting each token to a list of observers
wrapping each evaluation as an undoable command
questions.rationale: Interpreter models each grammar rule as a class and evaluates sentences built from them. The distractors describe Memento, Observer, and Command.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Behavioural module content
Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: Interpreter models each grammar rule as a class and evaluates sentences built from them. The distractors describe Memento, Observer, and Command.

questions.question: How is a sentence typically represented and evaluated in an Interpreter design?
options: As a single regular expression matched once
As one large switch statement over the raw text
As a tree of rule objects, with evaluation recursing over the tree by calling each node’s interpret
As a flat array of characters scanned left to right only
questions.rationale: A sentence becomes an abstract syntax tree of rule objects, and interpretation recurses over the tree, each node interpreting its children.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: A sentence becomes an abstract syntax tree of rule objects, and interpretation recurses over the tree, each node interpreting its children.

questions.question: Users type small filter expressions such as price > 10 AND inStock that nest arbitrarily, and you must evaluate them by composing rules recursively. Which approach fits?
options: Model each operator and operand as a class with an interpret method and compose them into a tree
Add a new visitor for each kind of filter without changing the operand classes
Pick the evaluation algorithm from a menu at runtime
Pass the expression through a chain of handlers until one evaluates it
questions.rationale: Representing each grammar element as a composable evaluable node and recursing is Interpreter. The distractors describe Visitor, Strategy, and Chain of Responsibility.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: Representing each grammar element as a composable evaluable node and recursing is Interpreter. The distractors describe Visitor, Strategy, and Chain of Responsibility.

options: Composite — a structural part-whole tree with no evaluation semantics
Strategy — an interchangeable algorithm
Observer — a subject notifying dependents
Interpreter — grammar-rule classes with eval composed into an expression tree
questions.rationale: Grammar-rule classes carrying eval and composed into an expression tree that evaluates a sentence is the Interpreter pattern.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: Grammar-rule classes carrying eval and composed into an expression tree that evaluates a sentence is the Interpreter pattern.

questions.question: A team adopts the Interpreter pattern to implement a large, performance-critical general-purpose programming language with deeply nested grammar. Is this an appropriate use, and why?
options: No — Interpreter suits small, stable grammars; a large, complex, performance-critical language is better served by parser/compiler tooling
Yes — Interpreter scales best to large languages
Yes — raw evaluation speed is Interpreter’s main strength
No — Interpreter is unable to evaluate any expression
questions.rationale: Interpreter fits small, stable grammars; for large complex performance-critical languages the class-per-rule tree becomes unwieldy and a parser/compiler is preferable.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Behavioural module content
Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: Interpreter fits small, stable grammars; for large complex performance-critical languages the class-per-rule tree becomes unwieldy and a parser/compiler is preferable.

questions.explanation: A creating-level check should produce code that the analyzer can recognize as Interpreter.

title: Memento
intro: Snapshot an object's state now so you can put it back later.

sections.heading: The shape in code
sections.body: The originator produces a Memento holding its state via save(), and restores from one via restore(). Only the originator can read the memento's contents.

summary: save()/restore() name the undo seam directly, so a reviewer finds the checkpoint boundary without hunting through field-by-field copies.

questions.question: The Memento pattern is used to ___.
options: coordinate communication among objects through a hub
select an algorithm at runtime
capture an object’s state so it can be restored later without exposing its internals
pass a request along a chain of handlers
questions.rationale: Memento captures and restores state without breaking encapsulation. The distractors describe Mediator, Strategy, and Chain of Responsibility.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Behavioural module content
Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: Memento captures and restores state without breaking encapsulation. The distractors describe Mediator, Strategy, and Chain of Responsibility.

questions.question: How does the Memento pattern preserve encapsulation while saving an object’s state?
options: It makes all of the object’s fields public so anyone can copy them
The originator produces an opaque memento that the caretaker stores without reading its contents
It exposes the state through getters available to every caller
It copies the originator’s class definition at runtime
questions.rationale: The originator builds the memento and the caretaker holds it opaquely, never inspecting the internals, so encapsulation is preserved.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: The originator builds the memento and the caretaker holds it opaquely, never inspecting the internals, so encapsulation is preserved.

questions.question: A text editor must support unlimited undo by saving the buffer’s state at each step and restoring an earlier one on demand, without exposing the buffer’s private fields. Which approach fits?
options: Have the buffer return an opaque snapshot a history keeper stores and hands back to restore it
Wrap each edit as a command and replay it in reverse
Give the buffer a current-state object that changes its behaviour
Notify a list of dependents whenever the buffer changes
questions.rationale: Producing an opaque snapshot stored by a caretaker and used to restore the object, without exposing internals, is Memento. The distractors lean toward Command, State, and Observer.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: Producing an opaque snapshot stored by a caretaker and used to restore the object, without exposing internals, is Memento. The distractors lean toward Command, State, and Observer.

options: Command — a reified request with execute/undo
State — behaviour changes with internal mode
Prototype — cloning via a virtual copy
Memento — an originator producing/consuming an opaque state snapshot only it can read
questions.rationale: An originator emitting an opaque snapshot (whose internals only it can read via friendship) and restoring from it is the Memento pattern.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: An originator emitting an opaque snapshot (whose internals only it can read via friendship) and restoring from it is the Memento pattern.

questions.question: A so-called snapshot design makes every internal field of an object public so external code can read them all, copy them, and later write them back directly. Does this honour Memento’s intent?
options: No — Memento captures state without breaking encapsulation; exposing all internals publicly violates that core intent
Yes — public fields are a requirement of Memento
Yes — any save-and-restore scheme is Memento
No — because Memento is unable to restore state at all
questions.rationale: Memento exists to capture state while keeping internals hidden; making every field public defeats the encapsulation the pattern is meant to protect.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Behavioural module content
Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns
questions.explanation: Memento exists to capture state while keeping internals hidden; making every field public defeats the encapsulation the pattern is meant to protect.

questions.explanation: A creating-level check should produce code that the analyzer can recognize as Memento.
